QUEUE requests may be received from socket clients or they may be received from LS2 processes in CPU n where n is the LCL array index. Socket APIs, write(), send(), sendto() and sendmsg(), update this counter when the sending and receiving sockets are mastered in different CPUs. (See Note under LS-SENDS counter.) ``` ``` Incrementing ``` ``` LS-QUEUE-BYTES Number of bytes received in QUEUE requests from CPU n where n is the LCL array index. Socket APIs, write(), send(), sendto() and sendmsg(), update this counter when the sending and receiving sockets are mastered in different CPUs. (See Note under LS-SENDS counter.) ``` ``` Accumulating ``` ``` LS-AWAKES Number of AWAKE operations performed on behalf of processes in CPU n where n is the LCL array index. Socket APIs, connect(), accept(), write(), send(), sendto(), sendmsg(), read(), recv(), recvfrom(), recvmsg(), sh utdown(), and close() update this counter. ``` ``` Incrementing ``` ``` LS-SELECTS Number of SELECT operations performed by the LS2 process to CPU n where n is the LCL array index. The socket API select() updates this counter. In addition, the procedures FILE_COMPLETE_() and FILE_COMPLETE_SET_() update this counter. ``` ``` Incrementing ``` ``` SubfieldSubfield DescriptionDescription Counter TypeCounter Type ``` These counters occur 16 times, once for each CPU in the system. # NFS-OPENSNFS-OPENS (ZMS Style only) In H06.25/J06.14 and later RVUs, current number of NFS opens (disk files that can be accessed using NFS) on a CPU. Counter type: Snapshot. # NFS-OPENS-LIMITNFS-OPENS-LIMIT (ZMS Style only) In H06.25/J06.14 and later RVUs, soft limit of NFS opens (disk files that can be accessed using NFS) on a CPU. See also Usage Notes for OSSCPU Entities. Counter type: Snapshot. # OSS-OPENSOSS-OPENS (ZMS Style only) In H06.25/J06.14 and later RVUs, current number of OSS file opens of all file types (regular file opens, pipe/FIFO opens, directory opens, directory streams, TTY opens, NFS opens, AF_INET socket opens, and AF_UNIX socket opens) on a CPU. Counter type: Snapshot. # OSS-OPENS-LIMITOSS-OPENS-LIMIT (ZMS Style only) In H06.25/J06.14 and later RVUs, limit of OSS file opens of all file types (regular file opens, pipe/FIFO opens, directory opens, directory streams, TTY opens, NFS opens, AF_INET socket opens, and AF_UNIX socket opens) on a CPU. Counter type: Snapshot. ``` NOTENOTE Regular file opens, directory opens, directory streams, and NFS opens are unbounded open resources used by the OSS file system. The DISK-OPENS-LIMIT, DIR-OPENS-LIMIT, DIR-STREAMS-LIMIT, and NFS- OPENS-LIMIT values represent soft limits. Until the OSS-OPENS-LIMIT value has been reached, all of these soft limits can be exceeded. (Also note, the OSS-OPENS-LIMIT counter and the ALL_OPENS resource limit in the OSS SCF interface represent the same value.) ``` # PIPE-FIFO-OPENSPIPE-FIFO-OPENS (ZMS Style only) In H06.25/J06.14 and later RVUs, current number of pipe and FIFO opens on a CPU. Counter type: Snapshot. # PIPE-FIFO-OPENS-LIMITPIPE-FIFO-OPENS-LIMIT (ZMS Style only) In H06.25/J06.14 and later RVUs, limit of pipe and FIFO opens on a CPU. Counter type: Snapshot. # PIPE-FIFO-BUF-BYTESPIPE-FIFO-BUF-BYTES (ZMS Style only) In H06.25/J06.14 and later RVUs, current number of bytes allocated for pipe and FIFO data buffers on a CPU. Counter type: Snapshot. # PIPE-FIFO-BUF-BYTES-LIMITPIPE-FIFO-BUF-BYTES-LIMIT (ZMS Style only) In H06.25/J06.14 and later RVUs, maximum size (in bytes) for pipe and FIFO data buffers on a CPU. Counter type: Snapshot. # PP-ALLOC-FAILURESPP-ALLOC-FAILURES Number of times an OSS pipe could not be resized or allocated. Counter type: Incrementing. # PS-GETTIME-REQSPS-GETTIME-REQS Number of gettime requests received by an OSS pipe server process from an OSS name server. Counter type: Incrementing. # PS-SETTIME-REQSPS-SETTIME-REQS Number of settime requests received by an OSS pipe server process from an OSS name server. Counter type: Incrementing. # PXS-BUF-BYTESPXS-BUF-BYTES (ZMS Style only) In H06.25/J06.14 and later RVUs, current number of bytes allocated within the POSIX extended segment (PXS) from 32-bit addressable memory on a CPU. Counter type: Snapshot. # PXS-BUF-BYTES-LIMITPXS-BUF-BYTES-LIMIT (ZMS Style only) In H06.25/J06.14 and later RVUs, maximum size (in bytes) for the POSIX extended segment (PXS) that is allocated from 32-bit addressable memory on a CPU. Counter type: Snapshot. # PXS-ENDING-BYTESPXS-ENDING-BYTES Current number of bytes allocated within the POSIX extended segment (PXS). Counter type: Snapshot. # PXS-FAILURESPXS-FAILURES Number of allocation failures encountered for the POSIX extension segment (PXS). An allocation failure can occur because of a lack of free space or a lack of contiguous free space. If the problem persists, user applications might need to be migrated to another CPU. Counter type: Incrementing. # PXS64-BUF-BYTESPXS64-BUF-BYTES (ZMS Style only) In H06.25/J06.14 and later RVUs, current number of bytes allocated within the POSIX extended segment (PXS64) from 64- bit addressable memory on a CPU. Counter type: Snapshot. # PXS64-BUF-BYTES-LIMITPXS64-BUF-BYTES-LIMIT (ZMS Style only) In H06.25/J06.14 and later RVUs, maximum size (in bytes) for the POSIX extended segment (PXS64) that is allocated from 64-bit addressable memory on a CPU. Counter type: Snapshot. # REMREM Number of OSS pipe server requests from system-remote pipe server processes. Measurement is divided into seven counters: ``` SubfieldSubfield DescriptionDescription Counter TypeCounter Type PS-PROXY-READS Number of proxy reads performed by an OSS pipe server process. Incrementing PS-PROXY-WRITES Number of proxy writes performed by an OSS pipe server process. Incrementing PS-PROXY-READ-BYTES Number of proxy read bytes read by an OSS pipe server process. Accumulating PS-PROXY-WRITE-BYTES Number of proxy write bytes written by an OSS pipe server process. Accumulating PS-SELECT-RCVD Number of select requests received by an OSS pipe server process from other systems. ``` ``` Incrementing ``` ``` PS-SELECT-SENT Number of select ready requests sent to an OSS pipe server process from other systems. ``` ``` Incrementing ``` ``` PS-SELECT-READY Number of select ready requests received by an OSS pipe server process from systems. ``` ``` Incrementing ``` These counters occur once for remote systems. # TTY-OPENSTTY-OPENS (ZMS Style only) In H06.25/J06.14 and later RVUs, current number of TTY opens (opens of tty devices using the /G name and the /dev/tty name) on a CPU. Counter type: Snapshot. # TTY-OPENS-LIMITTTY-OPENS-LIMIT (ZMS Style only) In H06.25/J06.14 and later RVUs, limit of TTY opens (opens of tty devices using the /G name and the /dev/tty name) on a CPU. Counter type: Snapshot. # Legacy Style Only Counter Field DDL Definitions for OSSCPU EntitiesLegacy Style Only Counter Field DDL Definitions for OSSCPU Entities These counter fields are used in Legacy Style DDL records only. Fields are listed alphabetically. SubtopicsSubtopics FS-CACHE-ACTIVE-QMAXFS-CACHE-ACTIVE-QMAX FS-CACHE-DIRTY-QMAXFS-CACHE-DIRTY-QMAX FS-CACHE-VALID-QMAXFS-CACHE-VALID-QMAX PP-BLOCK-INUSE-QMAXPP-BLOCK-INUSE-QMAX # FS-CACHE-ACTIVE-QMAXFS-CACHE-ACTIVE-QMAX (Measure G10 PVU only) Maximum number for active cache blocks in use. Counter type: Max queue. # FS-CACHE-DIRTY-QMAXFS-CACHE-DIRTY-QMAX (Measure G10 PVU only) Maximum value for dirty cache blocks. Counter type: Max queue. # FS-CACHE-VALID-QMAXFS-CACHE-VALID-QMAX (Measure G10 PVU only) Maximum value for cache blocks valid. Counter type: Max queue. # PP-BLOCK-INUSE-QMAXPP-BLOCK-INUSE-QMAX (Measure G10 PVU only) Maximum number of blocks in use by the OSS pipe pool. Counter type: Max queue. # Usage Notes for OSSCPU EntitiesUsage Notes for OSSCPU Entities ``` The following OSSCPU counter values represent the soft limits of unbounded open resources used by the OSS file system: DIR-OPENS-LIMIT DIR-STREAMS-LIMIT DISK-OPENS-LIMIT NFS-OPENS-LIMIT Unbounded open resources have soft limits that may be exceeded as long as the OSS-OPENS-LIMIT of 384,000 is not reached. (Note that the OSS-OPENS-LIMIT counter in Measure and the ALL_OPENS resource limit in the OSS SCF interface represent the same value.) For more information on OSS environmental limits, see the Open System Services Management and Operations Guide. A read or write operation on a regular file can be sent directly to DP2 or satisfied through the OSS file system cache. Noncached operations (FS-DIRECT-READS, FS-DIRECT-WRITES) result in messages to DP2 and transfer only as much information as specified by the client. Cached operations (FS-CACHE-READS, FS-CACHE-WRITES) utilize the file system cache in the client’s CPU and can result in a block read/write (FS-CACHE-RD-READ-REQS, FS-CACHE-WT-READ-REQS, FS-CACHE-WRITE-REQS) between the cache and DP2. Block reads and writes occur on cache block boundaries (except for the last cache block of the file) where a cache block is 4 KB. Block read operations try to read ahead (FS-PREFETCH-BLOCKS) to anticipate any need for the information in the immediate future. The OSS file system controls cache use and might, for internal reasons, select to bypass its use on a particular file open. ``` ``` When a pipe is remote from the sender or receiver, the pipe server in the CPU where the pipe was created acts as an agent for the remote clients. Write and read requests result in messages to the pipe server in the CPU where the pipe was created. The pipe server, in turn, performs proxy pipe reads and writes to the pipe buffer on behalf of the remote client. Select() messages between client and pipe server and between pipe servers, occur only when the client calling select() is running in a CPU other than where the pipe was created: 1. The client sends a select request to the pipe server that manages the pipe. 2. The pipe server replies immediately. 3. When the client’s read, write, or both can succeed, the pipe server sends a select ready request to the pipe server in the client’s CPU. 4. That pipe server awakens the client, and the select() call completes. ``` ``` 1 ``` ``` You can calculate the OSS cache miss rate as: ``` ``` Supported only on systems running L17.08/J06.22 and later RVUs. Prior to L17.08/J06.22 RVU, the limit was 128,000. ``` # OSSNSOSSNS The OSSNS entity provides information about the operation and performance of OSS name server processes. There is one record for the primary and backup processes configured for each OSS name server defined on a system. Any OSS operation involving an OSS file pathname uses the OSS name server. OSSNS entities can only be used on systems running Measure G10 and later PVUs. ``` TopicTopic Entity specification syntax for OSSNS entities DDL record for OSSNS entities DDL field definitions for OSSNS entities Usage notes for OSSNS entities ``` SubtopicsSubtopics Entity Specification Syntax for OSSNS EntitiesEntity Specification Syntax for OSSNS Entities DDL Record for OSSNS Entities (ZMS Style)DDL Record for OSSNS Entities (ZMS Style) DDL Record for OSSNS Entities (Legacy Style)DDL Record for OSSNS Entities (Legacy Style) Common ID Field DDL Definitions for OSSNS EntitiesCommon ID Field DDL Definitions for OSSNS Entities Common Counter Field DDL Definitions for OSSNS EntitiesCommon Counter Field DDL Definitions for OSSNS Entities Legacy Style Only Counter Field DDL Definitions for OSSNS EntitiesLegacy Style Only Counter Field DDL Definitions for OSSNS Entities Usage Notes for OSSNS EntitiesUsage Notes for OSSNS Entities # Entity Specification Syntax for OSSNS EntitiesEntity Specification Syntax for OSSNS Entities To describe OSSNS entities: entity-spec is specified as: ``` where: * ``` ``` OSS cache miss rate = (FS-Cache-RD-Read-Reqs + FS-Cache-WT-Read-Reqs + FS-Cache-Write-Reqs) ------------------------------------------------------------- (FS-Cache-Reads + FS-Cache-Writes) ``` ``` 1 ``` ``` OSSNS entity‑spec [, entity-spec ] ... ``` ``` { * } { pid } { $ process-name [( pid )] } { [[$ device .] subvolume .] filename [( pid )] } ``` ``` measures elements for all OSS name server processes on the local system. pid is the process identifier of the OSS name server process to be measured. Specify pid as one of: ``` ``` cpu is the number of the CPU on which the OSS name server process to be measured is running. To indicate all CPUs, use an asterisk (*). The default is all CPUs. pin is the process identification number of the OSS name server process to be measured. Use an asterisk (*) to specify all process identification numbers. SYSTEM ‑ PROCESSES measures all OSS name server processes installed using the SYSGEN program. process-name is the Guardian name of a specific OSS name server process to measure. $device is the device (volume) on which the object file of the OSS name server process to be measured is located. To indicate all volumes, use an asterisk (*). The default is the current default volume. subvolume is the subvolume in which the object file of the OSS name server process to be measured is located. To indicate all subvolumes, use an asterisk (*). filename is the name of the object file of the OSS name server process to be measured. To indicate all files (except temporary files), use an asterisk (*). ``` # DDL Record for OSSNS Entities (ZMS Style)DDL Record for OSSNS Entities (ZMS Style) The ZMS Style DDL record for OSSNS entities is supported in Measure G11 and later PVUs. The fields included in BRIEF reports are in boldface typeboldface type. SubtopicsSubtopics ID Fields DDL DefinitionID Fields DDL Definition Counter Fields DDL DefinitionCounter Fields DDL Definition DDL Record Description FieldsDDL Record Description Fields # ID Fields DDL DefinitionID Fields DDL Definition ``` { cpu , pin } { SYSTEM‑PROCESSES } ``` ``` DEFINITION zmsossns-id. 02 pin type binary 16 unsigned. 02 reserved-1 type character 6. 02 process-name type character 8. 02 program-file-name. 03 volume type character 8. 03 subvol type character 8. ``` # Counter Fields DDL DefinitionCounter Fields DDL Definition # DDL Record Description FieldsDDL Record Description Fields # DDL Record for OSSNS Entities (Legacy Style)DDL Record for OSSNS Entities (Legacy Style) The Legacy Style DDL record for OSSNS entities will not change after the Measure G10 PVU. The fields included in BRIEF reports are in boldface typeboldface type. ``` 03 filename type character 8. 02 IC-entries type binary 32 unsigned. 02 LC-entries type binary 32 unsigned. end ``` ``` DEFINITION zmsossns-ctrs. 02 RR-processed type binary 64. 02 RR-redir-sent type binary 64. 02 RR-redir-processed type binary 64. 02 IC-lookups type binary 64. 02 IC-hits type binary 64. 02 IC-dirty-qtime type binary 64. 02 LC-lookups type binary 64. 02 LC-hits type binary 64. 02 LC-inuse-qtime type binary 64. 02 Checkpoint-reqs type binary 64. 02 Checkpoint-blks type binary 64. 02 sem-waits type binary 64. 02 sem-wait-qtime type binary 64. 02 DP2-DD-reqs type binary 64. 02 DP2-messages type binary 64. 02 DP2-msg-qtime type binary 64. 02 PS-messages type binary 64. 02 PS-msg-qtime type binary 64. 02 LS-messages type binary 64. 02 LS-msg-qtime type binary 64. 02 gettime-reqs type binary 64. 02 settime-reqs type binary 64. end ``` ``` RECORD zmsossns. FILE is "zmsossns" ENTRY-SEQUENCED. 02 hdr type zmsheader. 02 ctr type zmsossns-ctrs. 02 id type zmsossns-id. end ``` ``` RECORD ossns. FILE is "ossns" ENTRY‑SEQUENCED. . . . ``` For descriptions of the header fields used by all entities, see Common Entity Header Fields. # Common ID Field DDL Definitions for OSSNS EntitiesCommon ID Field DDL Definitions for OSSNS Entities These ID fields are used in both styles of DDL records. Fields are listed alphabetically; subfields are listed in the order they occur in the field. SubtopicsSubtopics IC-ENTRIESIC-ENTRIES LC-ENTRIESLC-ENTRIES ``` (error, time items, and measurement identification items; see Common Entity Header Fields) . . . * Entity identification items: 02 pin type binary 16 unsigned. 02 process-name type character 8. 02 program-file-name. 03 volume type character 8. 03 subvol type character 8. 03 filename type character 8. 02 IC-entries type binary 32 unsigned. 02 LC-entries type binary 32 unsigned. *Counter value items: 02 RR-processed type binary 32 unsigned. 02 RR-redir-sent type binary 32 unsigned. 02 RR-redir-processed type binary 32 unsigned. 02 IC-lookups type binary 32 unsigned. 02 IC-hits type binary 32 unsigned. 02 IC-dirty-qtime type binary 64. 02 LC-lookups type binary 32 unsigned. 02 LC-hits type binary 32 unsigned. 02 LC-inuse-qtime type binary 64. 02 Checkpoint-reqs type binary 32 unsigned. 02 Checkpoint-blks type binary 32 unsigned. 02 Sem-waits type binary 32 unsigned. 02 Sem-wait-qtime type binary 64. 02 Sem-wait-qlen-max type binary 16 unsigned. 02 DP2-DD-reqs type binary 32 unsigned. 02 DP2-messages type binary 32 unsigned. 02 DP2-msg-qtime type binary 64. 02 DP2-msg-qlen-max type binary 16 unsigned. 02 PS-messages type binary 32 unsigned. 02 PS-msg-qtime type binary 64. 02 PS-msg-qlen-max type binary 16 unsigned. 02 LS-messages type binary 32 unsigned. 02 LS-msg-qtime type binary 64. 02 LS-msg-qlen-max type binary 16 unsigned. 02 gettime-reqs type binary 32 unsigned. 02 settime-reqs type binary 32 unsigned. end ``` ##### PINPIN ##### PROCESS-NAMEPROCESS-NAME ##### PROGRAM-FILE-NAMEPROGRAM-FILE-NAME # IC-ENTRIESIC-ENTRIES Configured number of inode cache records available on an OSS name server. # LC-ENTRIESLC-ENTRIES Configured number of link cache records available on an OSS name server. # PINPIN Process identification number of the measured OSS name server process. # PROCESS-NAMEPROCESS-NAME Name of the OSS name server process. # PROGRAM-FILE-NAMEPROGRAM-FILE-NAME Object file of the measured OSS name server process. The name can apply to either a Nonstop file or an SMF file. PROGRAM-FILE-NAME has three subfields: ``` VOLUME For Nonstop files, is the device location of the physical file. For SMF files, is the device location of the physical file that corresponds to the logical file name is stored in the DEVICE- NAME field. SUBVOLUME For Nonstop files, is the device name of the physical volume on which the program file is located. FILENAME For Nonstop files, represents the physical file name that was specified when the OSS name server process was opened. For SMF files, represents a location-independent logical file name that was used when the OSS name server process was opened. ``` # Common Counter Field DDL Definitions for OSSNS EntitiesCommon Counter Field DDL Definitions for OSSNS Entities These counter fields are used in both styles of DDL records. Fields are listed alphabetically. SubtopicsSubtopics CHECKPOINT-BLKSCHECKPOINT-BLKS CHECKPOINT-REQSCHECKPOINT-REQS DP2-DD-REQSDP2-DD-REQS DP2-MESSAGESDP2-MESSAGES DP2-MSG-QTIMEDP2-MSG-QTIME GETTIME-REQSGETTIME-REQS IC-DIRTY-QTIMEIC-DIRTY-QTIME IC-HITSIC-HITS IC-LOOKUPSIC-LOOKUPS LC-HITSLC-HITS LC-INUSE-QTIMELC-INUSE-QTIME LC-LOOKUPSLC-LOOKUPS LS-MESSAGESLS-MESSAGES LS-MSG-QTIMELS-MSG-QTIME PS-MESSAGESPS-MESSAGES PS-MSG-QTIMEPS-MSG-QTIME RR-PROCESSEDRR-PROCESSED RR-REDIR-PROCESSEDRR-REDIR-PROCESSED RR-REDIR-SENTRR-REDIR-SENT SEM-WAIT-QTIMESEM-WAIT-QTIME SEM-WAITSSEM-WAITS SETTIME-REQSSETTIME-REQS # CHECKPOINT-BLKSCHECKPOINT-BLKS Number of checkpoint blocks sent by an OSS name server to its backup. Counter type: Incrementing. # CHECKPOINT-REQSCHECKPOINT-REQS Number of logical checkpoint requests sent by an OSS name server. Checkpoint requests are assembled into blocks in the primary so that multiple requests are sent to the backup in each message. Counter type: Incrementing. # DP2-DD-REQSDP2-DD-REQS Number of data definition requests sent to DP2 by an OSS name server. Data definition operations are initiated by these procedures: ``` creat()link() mkdir()chmod()utime() bind() open()unlink()rmdir()chown() rename() ``` Counter type: Incrementing. # DP2-MESSAGESDP2-MESSAGES Number of messages an OSS name server sent to DP2. The total includes Data Definition calls, which modify the file label, as well as other calls that do not alter the disk entry. Counter type: Incrementing. # DP2-MSG-QTIMEDP2-MSG-QTIME Accumulated service time spent in a thread waiting while DP2 messages were outstanding on an OSS name server. Counter type: Queue. # GETTIME-REQSGETTIME-REQS Number of gettime requests sent by the OSS name server to a pipe server or local server. Counter type: Incrementing. # IC-DIRTY-QTIMEIC-DIRTY-QTIME Amount of time inode cache entries were dirty on an OSS name server. Counter type: Queue. # IC-HITSIC-HITS Number of inode cache hits encountered by an OSS name server. To derive inode cache misses, subtract IC-HITS from IC-LOOKUPS. Counter type: Incrementing. # IC-LOOKUPSIC-LOOKUPS Number of inode cache lookups performed by an OSS name server. Counter type: Incrementing. # LC-HITSLC-HITS Number of link cache hits encountered by an OSS name server. Link cache misses can be derived by subtracting LC-HITS from LC-LOOKUPS. Counter type: Incrementing. # LC-INUSE-QTIMELC-INUSE-QTIME Amount of time link cache entries were in use on an OSS name server. Link cache expands beyond the configured number of entries if needed. If expanded, the OSS name server process eventually scales link cache resources back to the configured size when demand lessens. Counter type: Queue. # LC-LOOKUPSLC-LOOKUPS Number of link cache lookups performed by an OSS name server. Counter type: Incrementing. # LS-MESSAGESLS-MESSAGES Number of messages an OSS name server sent to the local OSS server process. The local OSS server ($ZPLS for pre-AF_UNIX R2; $ZLS nn, where nn is the CPU number, or $ZLSNN for AF_UNIX R2) is the transport provider for AF_UNIX sockets. Counter type: Incrementing. # LS-MSG-QTIMELS-MSG-QTIME Amount of time spent waiting on completion of local server messages by an OSS name server. Counter type: Queue. # PS-MESSAGESPS-MESSAGES Number of messages an OSS name server sent to pipe server processes. The pipe server runs in each CPU ($ZPPnn, where nn = CPU number) and provides pipe services for applications using OSS pipes and FIFOs. Counter type: Incrementing. # PS-MSG-QTIMEPS-MSG-QTIME Amount of time pipe server messages from an OSS name server were outstanding. Counter type: Queue. # RR-PROCESSEDRR-PROCESSED Number of name resolution requests processed by the OSS name server under measurement. Counter type: Incrementing. # RR-REDIR-PROCESSEDRR-REDIR-PROCESSED Number of redirected name resolution requests processed by an OSS name server. Counter type: Incrementing. # RR-REDIR-SENTRR-REDIR-SENT Number of name resolution requests redirected by an OSS name server. Counter type: Incrementing. # SEM-WAIT-QTIMESEM-WAIT-QTIME Amount of time spent in a semaphore wait condition by OSS name server processing threads. An OSS name server is a multithreaded process. A semaphore wait is specific to a thread processing a name resolution request. Counter type: Queue. # SEM-WAITSSEM-WAITS Number of semaphore waits an OSS name server encounters in its processing threads. Counter type: Incrementing. # SETTIME-REQSSETTIME-REQS Number of settime requests sent by the OSS name server to a pipe server or local server. Counter type: Incrementing. # Legacy Style Only Counter Field DDL Definitions for OSSNS EntitiesLegacy Style Only Counter Field DDL Definitions for OSSNS Entities These counter fields are used in Legacy Style DDL records only. Fields are listed alphabetically. SubtopicsSubtopics DP2-MSG-QLEN-MAXDP2-MSG-QLEN-MAX ##### LS-MSG-QLEN-MAXLS-MSG-QLEN-MAX ##### PS-MSG-QLEN-MAXPS-MSG-QLEN-MAX ##### SEM-WAIT-QLEN-MAXSEM-WAIT-QLEN-MAX # DP2-MSG-QLEN-MAXDP2-MSG-QLEN-MAX (Measure G10 PVU only) Maximum number of DP2 messages outstanding for an OSS name server since it began execution. In H-series and later RVUs, this counter has a value of 1. Counter type: Max queue. # LS-MSG-QLEN-MAXLS-MSG-QLEN-MAX (Measure G10 PVU only) Maximum number of concurrent local server messages outstanding on an OSS name server since it began execution. In H-series and later RVUs, this counter has a value of 1. Counter type: Max queue. # PS-MSG-QLEN-MAXPS-MSG-QLEN-MAX (Measure G10 PVU only) Maximum number of concurrent pipe server messages outstanding on an OSS name server since it began execution. In H-series and later RVUs, this counter has a value of 1. Counter type: Max queue. # SEM-WAIT-QLEN-MAXSEM-WAIT-QLEN-MAX (Measure G10 PVU only) Maximum number of resolution request semaphore waits on an OSS name server since it began execution. In H-series and later RVUs, this counter has a value of 1. Counter type: Max queue. # Usage Notes for OSSNS EntitiesUsage Notes for OSSNS Entities ``` Fields that report received or redirected resolution requests reflect the capacity of an OSS name server to route requests to the appropriate resource. When a request is sent to a name server, the name server might be able to completely process the request, or it might require the services of another name server. In the latter case, the first name server replies to the client with a redirection reply, which causes the file-system library running in the client to compose and send a new redirected request to the second name server. ``` ``` Inode and link cache counters report on cache performance for OSS fileset catalog accesses. The OSS fileset catalog consists of three Guardian disk files: ``` ``` PXINODE A key-sequenced file containing one record for each directory, regular file, FIFO, and AF_UNIX socket. PXLINK A key-sequenced file containing one record for each file name in the fileset. PXLOG An unstructured file used to ensure catalog integrity after a failure. PXLOG is used only when the fileset is configured with BUFFERED NONE. ``` ``` To reduce the frequency of access to the PXINODE and PXLINK files, name servers cache records from these files in memory. Separate caches are used for each file. Cache size is reported in IC-ENTRIES and LC-ENTRIES. The size of the caches can be changed individually. Both the primary and backup name server processes have PXINODE caches. Only the primary process has a PXLINK cache. The PXLINK cache not only keeps copies of PXLINK records, it also remembers unsuccessful name lookups. If the name is looked up again, it is not necessary to reread the PXLINK file to discover again that the name does not exist. IC-ENTRIES and LC-ENTRIES are displayed as counters even though they are ID fields. ``` # PROCESSPROCESS The PROCESS entity provides information about one or more processes on a local system. In Measure G09 and later PVUs, the PROCESS entity type handles OSS file pathnames. ``` TopicTopic Entity specification syntax for PROCESS entities DDL record for PROCESS entities DDL field definitions for PROCESS entities Usage notes for PROCESS entities ``` SubtopicsSubtopics Entity Specification Syntax for PROCESS EntitiesEntity Specification Syntax for PROCESS Entities DDL Record for PROCESS Entities (ZMS Style)DDL Record for PROCESS Entities (ZMS Style) DDL Record for PROCESS Entities (Legacy Style)DDL Record for PROCESS Entities (Legacy Style) Common and ZMS Style Only ID Field DDL Definitions for PROCESS EntitiesCommon and ZMS Style Only ID Field DDL Definitions for PROCESS Entities Common and ZMS Style Only Counter Field DDL Definitions for PROCESS EntitiesCommon and ZMS Style Only Counter Field DDL Definitions for PROCESS Entities Legacy Style Only ID Field DDL Definitions for PROCESS EntitiesLegacy Style Only ID Field DDL Definitions for PROCESS Entities Legacy Style Only Counter Field DDL Definitions for PROCESS EntitiesLegacy Style Only Counter Field DDL Definitions for PROCESS Entities Usage Note for All PROCESS EntitiesUsage Note for All PROCESS Entities ``` Usage Notes for J-Series PROCESS EntitiesUsage Notes for J-Series PROCESS Entities Usage Notes for L-series PROCESS entitiesUsage Notes for L-series PROCESS entities Usage Notes for KLT processes for L-series PROCESS EntitiesUsage Notes for KLT processes for L-series PROCESS Entities Usage Notes for H-series and later PROCESS EntitiesUsage Notes for H-series and later PROCESS Entities Usage Notes for G-Series PROCESS EntitiesUsage Notes for G-Series PROCESS Entities ``` # Entity Specification Syntax for PROCESS EntitiesEntity Specification Syntax for PROCESS Entities To describe PROCESS entities: ##### PROCESS collects information about one or more processes on the local system. entity **_‑_** spec is specified as: ``` where: * measures all processes on the local system. pid is the process identifier of the process to be measured. Specify pid as one of: ``` ``` cpu is the number of the CPU on which the process to be measured is running. To indicate all CPUs, use an asterisk (*). The default is all CPUs. pin is the process identification number of the process to be measured. To indicate all processes, use an asterisk (*). The default is all processes. SYSTEM ‑ PROCESSES measures all processes installed using the SYSGEN program. $process ‑ name is the name of the process to be measured. Use an asterisk (*) to specify all processes. $device is the volume (device) on which the object file of the process to be measured is located. Use an asterisk (*) to indicate all volumes. The default is the current default volume. subvolume is the subvolume in which the object file of the process to be measured is located. Use an asterisk (*) to indicate all subvolumes. filename ``` ``` PROCESS entity‑spec [, entity-spec ] ... ``` ``` { * } { pid } { $ process‑name [ ( pid ) ] } { [[$ device .] subvolume .] filename [: crvsn ] [ ( pid ) ] } { " pname " [ ( pid ) ] } ``` ``` { cpu , pin } { SYSTEM‑PROCESSES } ``` ``` is the name of the object file of an executing process to be measured. Use an asterisk (*) to indicate all files (except temporary files). :crvsn (Measure G09 and later PVUs) is the timestamp, creation version serial number (CRVSN), or file name extension necessary to form a unique file name. To get the CRVSN for a file, see the Measure report or use the LISTGNAME command. "pname" (Measure G09 and later PVUs) is either a fully qualified or partial OSS file pathname. An OSS file pathname that does not begin with a slash (/) is considered to be a partial pathname and is expanded by prefacing it with the current setting for OSSPATH. An OSS file pathname that begins with /G is not supported, use the Guardian file name instead. NOTENOTE OSS file pathnames are case-sensitive and must be specified within double quotation marks (" "). Valid OSS file pathnames can refer to specific files or to a set of files within a specific directory. If a directory is specified, only files in that directory are included. Files in directories subordinate to the specified directory are not included. ``` # DDL Record for PROCESS Entities (ZMS Style)DDL Record for PROCESS Entities (ZMS Style) The ZMS Style DDL record for PROCESS entities is supported in Measure G11 and later PVUs. The fields included in BRIEF reports are in boldface typeboldface type. SubtopicsSubtopics ID Fields DDL DefinitionID Fields DDL Definition Counter Fields DDL DefinitionCounter Fields DDL Definition DDL Record Description FieldsDDL Record Description Fields # ID Fields DDL DefinitionID Fields DDL Definition ``` DEFINITION zmsproc-id. 02 pin type binary 16 unsigned. 02 priority type binary 16 unsigned. 02 userid. 03 group type binary 8 unsigned. 03 user type binary 8 unsigned. 02 creatorid. 03 group type binary 8 unsigned. 03 user type binary 8 unsigned. 02 process-name type character 8. 02 program-file-name. 03 volume type character 8. 03 subvol type character 8. 03 filename type character 8. 02 osspid type binary 32 unsigned. 02 ancestor-cpu type binary 16 unsigned. 02 ancestor-pin type binary 16 unsigned. 02 ancestor-sysname type character 8. 02 ancestor-process-name type character 8. 02 device-name type character 8. 02 program-file-name-MID. ``` # Counter Fields DDL DefinitionCounter Fields DDL Definition ``` 03 PATHID type character 24. 03 CRVSN type character 6. 02 flags type binary 16 unsigned. 02 GMOM. 03 GMOM-node type binary 16 unsigned. 03 GMOM-cpu type binary 16 unsigned. 03 GMOM-pin type binary 16 unsigned. 03 GMOM-jobid type binary 16 unsigned. 02 GMOM-full-id type binary 64 redefines GMOM. 02 GMOM-sysname type character 8. 02 GMOM-process-name type character 8. 02 program-accelerated type binary 16 unsigned. 02 native-process type binary 16 unsigned. 02 system-process type binary 16 unsigned. 02 ipus type binary 16 unsigned. 02 hometerm-sysname type character 8. 02 hometerm-name. 03 device type character 8. 03 subdevice type character 8. 03 qualifier type character 8. 02 serverclass-name type character 32. * New id fields for L03 02 thread-group-id type binary 32 unsigned. 02 reserved-1 type character 52. end ``` ``` DEFINITION zmsproc-ctrs. 02 cpu‑busy‑time type binary 64. 02 ready‑time type binary 64. 02 mem‑qtime type binary 64. 02 dispatches type binary 64. 02 page‑faults type binary 64. 02 pres‑pages‑qtime type binary 64. 02 ext‑segs‑qtime type binary 64. 02 vsems type binary 64. 02 recv‑qtime type binary 64. 02 messages‑sent type binary 64. 02 sent‑bytes type binary 64. 02 returned‑bytes type binary 64. 02 messages‑received type binary 64. 02 received‑bytes type binary 64. 02 reply‑bytes type binary 64. 02 mqc‑allocations type binary 64 02 mqc‑alloc‑failures type binary 64 02 mqcs‑inuse‑qtime type binary 64 02 checkpoints type binary 64. 02 comp‑traps type binary 64. 02 native-busy-time type binary 64. 02 accel‑busy‑time type binary 64. 02 tns‑busy‑time type binary 64. 02 alloc‑seg‑calls type binary 64. ``` 02 file‑open‑calls type binary 64. 02 info‑calls type binary 64. 02 begin‑trans type binary 64. 02 abort‑trans type binary 64. 02 pres-pages-start type binary 32 unsigned. 02 pres-pages-end type binary 32 unsigned. 02 Abs-segs-qtime type binary 64. 02 Abs-segs-start type binary 32 unsigned. 02 Abs-segs-end type binary 32 unsigned. 02 Msgs-sent-qtime type binary 64. 02 Sent-cbytes type binary 64. 02 Returned-cbytes type binary 64. 02 Received-cbytes type binary 64. 02 Reply-cbytes type binary 64. 02 OSS-tty-reads type binary 64. 02 OSS-tty-writes type binary 64. 02 OSS-tty-read-bytes type binary 64. 02 OSS-tty-write-bytes type binary 64. 02 OSS-tty-wait-time type binary 64. 02 OSS-dev-null-ops type binary 64. 02 OSSNS-DD-calls type binary 64. 02 OSSNS-requests type binary 64. 02 OSSNS-message-bytes type binary 64. 02 OSSNS-wait-time type binary 64. 02 OSSNS-redirects type binary 64. 02 launches type binary 64. 02 launch-wait-time type binary 64. 02 open-close-wait-time type binary 64. 02 ipu-switches type binary 64. * New counters for H05: 02 ipu-num type binary 32 unsigned. 02 ipu-num-prev type binary 32 unsigned. * New counters for H06 02 initial-priority-start type binary 16. 02 initial-priority-end type binary 16. 02 current-priority-start type binary 16. 02 current-priority-end type binary 16. 02 locked-pages-start type binary 64. 02 locked-pages-end type binary 64. * New counters for H07 02 X-interrupts type binary 64. 02 Y-interrupts type binary 64. 02 Interrupts type binary 64. 02 mainstack-size-start type binary 64. 02 mainstack-size-end type binary 64. 02 mainstack-limit-start type binary 64. 02 mainstack-limit-end type binary 64. 02 privstack-size-start type binary 64. 02 privstack-size-end type binary 64. 02 privstack-limit type binary 64. 02 heap32-size-start type binary 64. 02 heap32-size-end type binary 64. 02 heap32-limit type binary 64. # DDL Record Description FieldsDDL Record Description Fields # DDL Record for PROCESS Entities (Legacy Style)DDL Record for PROCESS Entities (Legacy Style) The Legacy Style DDL record for PROCESS entities will not change after the Measure G10 PVU. The fields included in BRIEF reports are in boldface typeboldface type. The DDL record for G-series PROCESS entities is identical to the record for D-series PROCESS entities except: Longer byte‑count fields are provided to reduce the possibility of field overflow. Each 32‑bit byte‑count field has a 64‑bit counterpart. For information on using the 64-bit fields, see Usage Notes for G-Series PROCESS Entities. The ERROR field can signal a field overflow in a 32‑bit byte‑count field. Counters are added to support operating system changes in memory handling. The memory handling changes also affect the interpretation of the existing CPU-BUSY-TIME, READY-TIME, and PRES-PAGES-QTIME counters. In Measure G09 and later PVUs, several identifiers are added for OSS file pathname support. ``` 02 heap64-size-start type binary 64. 02 heap64-size-end type binary 64. 02 heap64-limit type binary 64. 02 globals-size type binary 64. 02 swapspace type binary 64. 02 tles-start type binary 64. 02 tles-end type binary 64. 02 ipu-affinity-class-start type binary 16 unsigned 02 ipu-affinity-class-end type binary 16 unsigned * New counter for L02 02 reserved-2 type character 4. 02 checkpoint-busy-time type binary 64. * New counters for L03 02 process-launches type binary 64. 02 process-launch-qtime type binary 64. 02 thread-launches type binary 64. 02 thread-launch-qtime type binary 64. 02 file-awaitio-calls type binary 64. 02 file-awaitio-qtime type binary 64. 02 file-nowait-completed type binary 64. 02 file-nowait-initiated type binary 64. 02 file-reads type binary 64. 02 file-writes type binary 64. 02 open-closes type binary 64. 02 reserved-3 type character 80. end ``` ``` RECORD zmsproc. FILE is "zmsproc" ENTRY-SEQUENCED. 02 hdr type zmsheader. 02 ctr type zmsproc-ctrs. 02 id type zmsproc-id. end ``` RECORD process. FILE is "process" ENTRY‑SEQUENCED. . . . (error, time items, and measurement identification items; see Common Entity Header Fields) . . . * entity identification items: 02 pin type binary 16 unsigned. 02 process‑name type character 8. 02 program‑file‑name. 03 volume type character 8. 03 subvol type character 8. 03 filename type character 8. 02 priority type binary 16 unsigned. * counter value items: **02 cpu‑busy‑time type binary 64. 02 ready‑time type binary 64.** 02 mem‑qtime type binary 64. 02 dispatches type binary 32 unsigned. 02 page‑faults type binary 32 unsigned. 02 pres‑pages‑qtime type binary 64. 02 pres‑pages‑max type binary 16 unsigned. 02 ext‑segs‑qtime type binary 64. 02 ext‑segs‑max type binary 16 unsigned. 02 vsems type binary 32 unsigned. **02 recv‑qtime type binary 64. 02 recv‑qlen‑max type binary 16 unsigned. 02 messages‑sent type binary 32 unsigned.** 02 sent‑bytes type binary 32 unsigned. 02 returned‑bytes type binary 32 unsigned. **02 messages‑received type binary 32 unsigned.** 02 received‑bytes type binary 32 unsigned. 02 reply‑bytes type binary 32 unsigned. 02 lcb‑allocations type binary 32 unsigned. 02 mqc‑allocations type binary 32 unsigned redefines lcb‑allocations. 02 lcb‑alloc‑failures type binary 32 unsigned. 02 mqc‑alloc‑failures type binary 32 unsigned redefines lcb‑alloc‑failures. 02 lcbs‑inuse‑qtime type binary 64. 02 mqcs‑inuse‑qtime type binary 64 redefines lcbs‑inuse‑qtime. 02 max‑lcbs‑inuse type binary 16 unsigned. 02 max‑mqcs‑inuse type binary 16 unsigned redefines max‑lcbs‑inuse. 02 checkpoints type binary 32 unsigned. * new fields for entity identification: 02 userid. 03 group type binary 8 unsigned. 03 user type binary 8 unsigned. 02 creatorid. 03 group type binary 8 unsigned. 03 user type binary 8 unsigned. * fields for TNS/R specific counters: 02 accel‑busy‑time type binary 64. 02 tns‑busy‑time type binary 64. 02 comp‑traps type binary 32 unsigned. 02 program‑accelerated type binary 16 unsigned. * new entity identification items for D10 02 ancestor‑cpu type binary 16 unsigned. 02 ancestor‑pin type binary 16 unsigned. 02 ancestor‑sysname type character 8. 02 ancestor‑process‑name type character 8. * Native Mode busy time: 02 tnsr‑busy‑time type binary 64. * Native Mode process: 02 tnsr‑process type binary 16 unsigned. * new entity identification items and counters for D25 02 hometerm‑sysname type character 8. 02 hometerm‑name. 03 device type character 8. 03 subdevice type character 8. 03 qualifier type character 8. 02 page‑size‑bytes type binary 16 unsigned. 02 alloc‑seg‑calls type binary 32 unsigned. 02 UCL‑qtime type binary 64. 02 UCL‑max type binary 16 unsigned. 02 UCL-lock-qtime type binary 64. 02 UCL-lock-max type binary 16 unsigned. 02 file‑open‑calls type binary 32 unsigned. 02 info‑calls type binary 32 unsigned. * D30 changes: 02 begin‑trans type binary 32 unsigned. 02 abort‑trans type binary 32 unsigned. * SMS changes: 02 device-name type character 8. * F40 new counter value items: 02 sent‑bytes‑f type binary 64. 02 returned‑bytes‑f type binary 64. 02 received‑bytes‑f type binary 64. 02 reply‑bytes‑f type binary 64. * New counters for G04 02 pres-pages-start type binary 32 unsigned. 02 pres-pages-end type binary 32 unsigned. 02 abs-segs-qtime type binary 64. 02 abs-segs-qlen-max type binary 16 unsigned. 02 abs-segs-start type binary 32 unsigned. 02 abs-segs-end type binary 32 unsigned. * New counters for G08: 02 msgs-sent-qtime type binary 64. 02 msgs-sent-qlen-max type binary 16 unsigned. 02 sent-cbytes type binary 64. 02 returned-cbytes type binary 64. 02 received-cbytes type binary 64. 02 reply-cbytes type binary 64. * New identifiers for OSS file pathname support: 02 osspid type binary 32 unsigned. For descriptions of the header fields used by all entities, see Common Entity Header Fields. # Common and ZMS Style Only ID Field DDL Definitions for PROCESS EntitiesCommon and ZMS Style Only ID Field DDL Definitions for PROCESS Entities These ID fields are used in both styles of DDL records. Fields that are used in ZMS Style DDL records only are noted. Fields are listed alphabetically; subfields are listed in the order they occur in the field. SubtopicsSubtopics ANCESTOR-CPUANCESTOR-CPU ANCESTOR-PINANCESTOR-PIN ANCESTOR-PROCESS-NAMEANCESTOR-PROCESS-NAME ANCESTOR-SYSNAMEANCESTOR-SYSNAME CREATORIDCREATORID DEVICE-NAMEDEVICE-NAME FLAGSFLAGS GMOMGMOM GMOM-FULL-IDGMOM-FULL-ID GMOM-PROCESS-NAMEGMOM-PROCESS-NAME GMOM-SYSNAMEGMOM-SYSNAME HOMETERM-NAMEHOMETERM-NAME HOMETERM-SYSNAMEHOMETERM-SYSNAME IPUSIPUS NATIVE-PROCESSNATIVE-PROCESS ``` 02 program-file-name-MID. 03 PATHID type character 24. 03 CRVSN type character 6. * New identifiers for NetBatch Job Control: 02 GMOM. 03 GMOM-node type binary 16 unsigned. 03 GMOM-cpu type binary 16 unsigned. 03 GMOM-pin type binary 16 unsigned. 03 GMOM-jobid type binary 16 unsigned. 02 GMOM-full-id type binary 64 redefines GMOM. 02 GMOM-sysname type character 8. 02 GMOM-process-name type character 8. * New counters for G10: 02 OSS-tty-reads type binary 32 unsigned. 02 OSS-tty-writes type binary 32 unsigned. 02 OSS-tty-read-bytes type binary 64. 02 OSS-tty-write-bytes type binary 64. 02 OSS-tty-wait-time type binary 64. 02 OSS-dev-null-ops type binary 64. 02 OSSNS-DD-calls type binary 32 unsigned. 02 OSSNS-requests type binary 32 unsigned. 02 OSSNS-message-bytes type binary 64. 02 OSSNS-wait-time type binary 64. 02 OSSNS-redirects type binary 32 unsigned. 02 launches type binary 32 unsigned. 02 launch-wait-time type binary 64. 02 open-close-wait-time type binary 64. end ``` ##### OSSPIDOSSPID ##### PINPIN ##### PRIORITYPRIORITY ##### PROCESS-NAMEPROCESS-NAME ##### PROGRAM-ACCELERATEDPROGRAM-ACCELERATED ##### PROGRAM-FILE-NAMEPROGRAM-FILE-NAME ##### PROGRAM-FILE-NAME-MIDPROGRAM-FILE-NAME-MID ##### SERVERCLASS-NAMESERVERCLASS-NAME ##### SYSTEM-PROCESSSYSTEM-PROCESS ##### THREAD-GROUP-IDTHREAD-GROUP-ID ##### USERIDUSERID # ANCESTOR-CPUANCESTOR-CPU Number of the CPU on which the ancestor process resides. # ANCESTOR-PINANCESTOR-PIN Process identification number of the ancestor process. # ANCESTOR-PROCESS-NAMEANCESTOR-PROCESS-NAME Name of the ancestor process. The report header displays the ancestor information. If this information cannot be obtained, the string “unknown” appears. # ANCESTOR-SYSNAMEANCESTOR-SYSNAME Name of the system on which the ancestor process resides. # CREATORIDCREATORID Creator access ID (CAID). This field identifies the creator of the process. It is divided into two subfields: GROUP and USER. # DEVICE-NAMEDEVICE-NAME Disk device on which the program file is located. For SMF files, this field provides the physical location that corresponds to the logical program file name. For Nonstop files, this field is the same as the PROGRAM-FILE-NAME VOLUME subfield. # FLAGSFLAGS HPE Nonstop Measure Reference Manual 431 # FLAGSFLAGS (ZMS style only) A 16-bit field that is used to indicate information about the process. flags.0:09 Reserved for future use. flags.10 1 = The process is a KLT Process *** flags.11 1 = The process is an Auxiliary Process (AP) ** flags.12 1 = The process handles X and Y interrupts ** flags.13 1 = The process is an Interrupt Process (IP) ** flags.14 Reserved for future use. flags.15 1 = The measurement was done on an NSMA system * * H06.24/J06.13 and later RVUs ** L01, H07/J05, and later PVUs *** L03 and later PVUs # GMOMGMOM The GMOM structure consists of four subfields: ``` GMOM-NODE Expand node number of the GMOM process if this process is part of a NetBatch job and the GMOM process is remote; otherwise zero. GMOM-CPU Processor number of the GMOM process if this process is part of a NetBatch job; otherwise zero. GMOM-PIN PIN of the GMOM process if this process is part of a NetBatch job; otherwise zero. GMOM-JOBID Job ID of the NetBatch job that initiated this process, assigned by the indicated GMOM; otherwise zero. ``` # GMOM-FULL-IDGMOM-FULL-ID 64-bit redefinition of the individual GMOM fields as a single value. A value of zero indicates that the process is not part of a NetBatch job stream. # GMOM-PROCESS-NAMEGMOM-PROCESS-NAME Name of the GMOM process that initiated the NetBatch job that initiated this process; otherwise spaces. # GMOM-SYSNAMEGMOM-SYSNAME Expand system name of the GMOM node if the GMOM process is remote; otherwise spaces. # HOMETERM-NAMEHOMETERM-NAME Home terminal associated with the process, identified by subcomponent (DEVICE name, SUBDEVICE name, and QUALIFIER name). This identifier appears only in the data record, not in the PROCESS report. # HOMETERM-SYSNAMEHOMETERM-SYSNAME Home terminal system name associated with the process. This identifier appears only in the data record, not in the PROCESS report. # IPUSIPUS (ZMS Style only) In Measure J01 and later PVUs, indicates the number of IPUs in the CPU. The field is not displayed by MEASCOM, but determines whether the IPU-SWITCHES field is displayed. In Measure H03 and later H-series PVUs, this field is 1, and is not displayed by MEASCOM. # NATIVE-PROCESSNATIVE-PROCESS (ZMS Style only) A flag set for processes executing only in native mode. # OSSPIDOSSPID If the process runs in the OSS environment, this field is the OSS Process ID. For a non-OSS process, the OSSPID contains zeros. # PINPIN Process identification number of the measured process. # PRIORITYPRIORITY Creation priority of the measured process. Changing a process priority using the ALTPRI TACL command does not affect this value. # PROCESS-NAMEPROCESS-NAME Name of the process. # PROGRAM-ACCELERATEDPROGRAM-ACCELERATED A flag that is set to nonzero if the user code (UC) or user library (UL) portions of the program have been accelerated. # PROGRAM-FILE-NAMEPROGRAM-FILE-NAME Object file of the measured process. The name can apply to either a Nonstop file or an SMF file. PROGRAM-FILE-NAME has three subfields: ``` VOLUME For Nonstop files, is the device location of the physical file. For SMF files, is the device location of the physical file that corresponds to the logical file name is stored in the DEVICE- NAME field. SUBVOLUME For Nonstop files, is the device name of the physical volume on which the program file is located. FILENAME For Nonstop files, represents the physical file name that was specified when the process was opened. For SMF files, represents a location-independent logical file name that was used when the process was opened. ``` # PROGRAM-FILE-NAME-MIDPROGRAM-FILE-NAME-MID Internal representation of an OSS file name for the specified PROGRAM-FILE-NAME. PROGRAM-FILE-NAME-MID has two subfields: ``` PATHID is an internal representation of an OSS file name for the specified PROGRAM-FILE-NAME. For non-OSS files, this field contains zeros. CRVSN is the creation version serial number that identifies a unique instance of an OSS file. For other files, this field contains zeros. ``` # SERVERCLASS-NAMESERVERCLASS-NAME (ZMS style only, L02, J06 and later) Serverclass name of the process. This field will be displayed if populated by Nonstop TS/MP pathway. # SYSTEM-PROCESSSYSTEM-PROCESS (ZMS style only, L01,H07/J05 and later) This field identifies whether the process is a system process, and if so, the system process type. SYSPROC_TYPE_REGULAR 0 Not a system process SYSPROC_TYPE_MONITOR 1 Monitor SYSPROC_TYPE_MEMMGR 2 Memory manager SYSPROC_TYPE_MESSENGER 3 Messenger SYSPROC_TYPE_MEASURE_IP 4 Measure IP SYSPROC_TYPE_IBRKPT_IP 5 Instruction Breakpoint Interrupt Process SYSPROC_TYPE_TIMER_IP 6 Timer IP SYSPROC_TYPE_MAB_IP 7 Memory Access Breakpoint Interrupt Process SYSPROC_TYPE_GFH 8 General Fault Handler SYSPROC_TYPE_SIGTRAP_MONITOR 9 Signal Trap Monitor SYSPROC_TYPE_IDLE_AP 10 Idle Auxiliary Process SYSPROC_TYPE_ALIVE_AP 11 Alive Auxiliary Process SYSPROC_TYPE_WATCHDOG_AP 12 Watchdog Timer Auxiliary Process SYSPROC_TYPE_SUSP_ACT_MON_AP 13 Suspend/Activate Monitor Auxiliary Process SYSPROC_TYPE_IB_MISC_IP 14 L-series Miscellaneous Interrupt Process SYSPROC_TYPE_IB_DRIVER_IP 15 L-series Driver Interrupt Process SYSPROC_TYPE_IB_COMM_IP 16 L-series Comm Interrupt Process SYSPROC_TYPE_IB_STORAGE_IP 17 L-series Storage Interrupt Process SYSPROC_TYPE_IB_IPC_IP 18 L-series Message System Interrupt Process SYSPROC_TYPE_IB_ST_WQ_AP 19 L-series Single-Threaded Work Queue Auxiliary Process SYSPROC_TYPE_IB_MT_WQ_AP 20 L-series Multi-Threaded Work Queue Auxiliary Process SYSPROC_TYPE_IB_HELPER_AP 21 L-series Helper Auxiliary Process SYSPROC_TYPE_IB_LNX_HELPER_AP 22 L-series Linux Timer Helper Auxiliary Process SYSPROC_TYPE_IMIP_AP 23 L-series Maintenance Interface Protocol Auxiliary Process SYSPROC_TYPE_TNET_COMM_IP 24 Servernet Comm Interrupt Process SYSPROC_TYPE_TNET_STORAGE_IP 25 Servernet Storage Interrupt Process SYSPROC_TYPE_TNET_IPC_IP 26 Servernet Message System Interrupt Process SYSPROC_TYPE_TNET_ERROR_IP 27 Servernet Error Interrupt Process SYSPROC_TYPE_TNET_TIMER_AP 28 Servernet Timer Auxiliary Process SYSPROC_TYPE_SMIP_IP 29 Servernet Maintenance Interface Protocol Interrupt Process SYSPROC_TYPE_SMIP_AP 30 Servernet Maintenance Interface Protocol Auxiliary Process SYSPROC_TYPE_IB_NSADI_IP 31 L-series Application Direct Interface Interrupt Process # THREAD-GROUP-IDTHREAD-GROUP-ID (ZMS Style only) This counter is supported in Measure L03 and later PVUs. This counter is valid only in the KLT process. Otherwise, the counter contains a zero value. # USERIDUSERID Process accessor ID (PAID) of the process. # Common and ZMS Style Only Counter Field DDL Definitions for PROCESS EntitiesCommon and ZMS Style Only Counter Field DDL Definitions for PROCESS Entities These counter fields are used in both styles of DDL records. Fields that are used in ZMS Style DDL records only are noted. Fields are listed alphabetically. SubtopicsSubtopics ``` ABORT-TRANSABORT-TRANS ABS-SEGS-ENDABS-SEGS-END ABS-SEGS-QTIMEABS-SEGS-QTIME ABS-SEGS-STARTABS-SEGS-START ACCEL-BUSY-TIMEACCEL-BUSY-TIME ALLOC-SEG-CALLSALLOC-SEG-CALLS BEGIN-TRANSBEGIN-TRANS CHECKPOINTSCHECKPOINTS CHECKPOINT-BUSY-TIMECHECKPOINT-BUSY-TIME COMP-TRAPSCOMP-TRAPS CPU-BUSY-TIMECPU-BUSY-TIME CURRENT-PRIORITY-ENDCURRENT-PRIORITY-END CURRENT-PRIORITY-STARTCURRENT-PRIORITY-START DISPATCHESDISPATCHES EXT-SEGS-QTIMEEXT-SEGS-QTIME FILE-AWAITIO-ARTFILE-AWAITIO-ART FILE-AWAITIO-CALLSFILE-AWAITIO-CALLS FILE-AWAITIO-QTIMEFILE-AWAITIO-QTIME FILE-NOWAIT-COMPLETEDFILE-NOWAIT-COMPLETED FILE-NOWAIT-INITIATEDFILE-NOWAIT-INITIATED FILE-OPEN-CALLSFILE-OPEN-CALLS FILE-READSFILE-READS FILE-WRITESFILE-WRITES GLOBALS-SIZEGLOBALS-SIZE HEAP32-LIMITHEAP32-LIMIT HEAP32-SIZE-ENDHEAP32-SIZE-END HEAP32-SIZE-STARTHEAP32-SIZE-START HEAP64-LIMITHEAP64-LIMIT HEAP64-SIZE-ENDHEAP64-SIZE-END HEAP64-SIZE-STARTHEAP64-SIZE-START INFO-CALLSINFO-CALLS ``` ##### INITIAL-PRIORITY-ENDINITIAL-PRIORITY-END ##### INITIAL-PRIORITY-STARTINITIAL-PRIORITY-START ##### INTERRUPTSINTERRUPTS ##### IPU-AFFINITY-CLASS-ENDIPU-AFFINITY-CLASS-END ##### IPU-AFFINITY-CLASS-STARTIPU-AFFINITY-CLASS-START ##### IPU-NUMIPU-NUM ##### IPU-NUM-PREVIPU-NUM-PREV ##### IPU-SWITCHESIPU-SWITCHES ##### LAUNCH-WAIT-TIMELAUNCH-WAIT-TIME ##### LAUNCHESLAUNCHES ##### LOCKED-PAGES-ENDLOCKED-PAGES-END ##### LOCKED-PAGES-STARTLOCKED-PAGES-START ##### MAINSTACK-LIMIT-ENDMAINSTACK-LIMIT-END ##### MAINSTACK-LIMIT-STARTMAINSTACK-LIMIT-START ##### MAINSTACK-SIZE-ENDMAINSTACK-SIZE-END ##### MAINSTACK-SIZE-STARTMAINSTACK-SIZE-START ##### MEM-QTIMEMEM-QTIME ##### MESSAGES-RECEIVEDMESSAGES-RECEIVED ##### MESSAGES-SENTMESSAGES-SENT ##### MQC-ALLOC-FAILURESMQC-ALLOC-FAILURES ##### MQC-ALLOCATIONSMQC-ALLOCATIONS ##### MQCS-INUSE-QTIMEMQCS-INUSE-QTIME ##### MSGS-SENT-QTIMEMSGS-SENT-QTIME ##### NATIVE-BUSY-TIMENATIVE-BUSY-TIME ##### OPEN-CLOSE-WAIT-TIMEOPEN-CLOSE-WAIT-TIME ##### OPEN-CLOSESOPEN-CLOSES ##### OSS-DEV-NULL-OPSOSS-DEV-NULL-OPS ##### OSS-TTY-READ-BYTESOSS-TTY-READ-BYTES ##### OSS-TTY-READSOSS-TTY-READS ##### OSS-TTY-WAIT-TIMEOSS-TTY-WAIT-TIME ##### OSS-TTY-WRITE-BYTESOSS-TTY-WRITE-BYTES ##### OSS-TTY-WRITESOSS-TTY-WRITES ##### OSSNS-ARTOSSNS-ART ##### OSSNS-DD-CALLSOSSNS-DD-CALLS ##### OSSNS-MESSAGE-BYTESOSSNS-MESSAGE-BYTES ##### OSSNS-REDIRECTSOSSNS-REDIRECTS ##### OSSNS-REQUESTSOSSNS-REQUESTS ##### OSSNS-WAIT-TIMEOSSNS-WAIT-TIME ##### PAGE-FAULTSPAGE-FAULTS ##### PRES-PAGES-ENDPRES-PAGES-END ##### PRES-PAGES-QTIMEPRES-PAGES-QTIME ##### PRES-PAGES-STARTPRES-PAGES-START ##### PRIVSTACK-LIMITPRIVSTACK-LIMIT ##### PRIVSTACK-SIZE-ENDPRIVSTACK-SIZE-END ##### PRIVSTACK-SIZE-STARTPRIVSTACK-SIZE-START ##### PROCESS-LAUNCH-ARTPROCESS-LAUNCH-ART ##### PROCESS-LAUNCHESPROCESS-LAUNCHES ##### PROCESS-LAUNCH-QTIMEPROCESS-LAUNCH-QTIME ##### READY-TIMEREADY-TIME ##### RECEIVED-BYTESRECEIVED-BYTES ##### RECEIVED-CBYTESRECEIVED-CBYTES ##### RECV-QTIMERECV-QTIME ##### REPLY-BYTESREPLY-BYTES ##### REPLY-CBYTESREPLY-CBYTES ##### RETURNED-BYTESRETURNED-BYTES ##### RETURNED-CBYTESRETURNED-CBYTES ##### SENT-BYTESSENT-BYTES ##### SENT-CBYTESSENT-CBYTES ##### SWAPSPACESWAPSPACE ##### THREAD-LAUNCH-ARTTHREAD-LAUNCH-ART ##### THREAD-LAUNCHESTHREAD-LAUNCHES ##### THREAD-LAUNCH-QTIMETHREAD-LAUNCH-QTIME ##### TLES-ENDTLES-END ##### TLES-STARTTLES-START ##### TNS-BUSY-TIMETNS-BUSY-TIME ##### VSEMSVSEMS ##### X-INTERRUPTSX-INTERRUPTS ##### Y-INTERRUPTSY-INTERRUPTS # ABORT-TRANSABORT-TRANS Number of times the process invokes the TMF ABORTTRANSACTION procedure to abort the current transaction. The number of calls does not necessarily correspond to the number of backouts driven by the TMP as recorded in the TMF entity. For example, if the application aborted the transaction without performing any transaction work, a backout is not required. Counter type: Incrementing. # ABS-SEGS-ENDABS-SEGS-END Number of items on the queue described by ABS-SEGS-QTIME at the end of the measurement interval. Counter type: Snapshot. # ABS-SEGS-QTIMEABS-SEGS-QTIME Time that segment table entries were in use for absolute addressable segments on behalf of the measured process. Counter type: Queue. # ABS-SEGS-STARTABS-SEGS-START Number of items on the queue described by ABS-SEGS-QTIME at the start of the measurement interval. Counter type: Snapshot. # ACCEL-BUSY-TIMEACCEL-BUSY-TIME Time that the CPU was busy executing accelerated code in this process. This counter applies only to measurements taken on a TNS/R or TNS/E system. See Usage Notes for H-series and later PROCESS Entities. Counter type: Busy. # ALLOC-SEG-CALLSALLOC-SEG-CALLS Number of successful calls made to the SEGMENT_ALLOCATE_ family of APIs. Counter type: Incrementing. # BEGIN-TRANSBEGIN-TRANS Number of times the process began a TMF transaction. Counter type: Incrementing. # CHECKPOINTSCHECKPOINTS Number of calls to the CHECKPOINT family of APIs. This counter includes both user and system calls to this procedure. Many system processes do their own checking rather than calling a procedure of the CHECKPOINT family of APIs. These processes do not increment this counter. This counter is not instrumented or displayed for KLT processes. Counter type: Incrementing. # CHECKPOINT-BUSY-TIMECHECKPOINT-BUSY-TIME (ZMS Style only) Time spent in checkpointing by this process. For waited checkpoints, the CHECKPOINT-BUSY-TIME counter is the elapsed time spent executing waited checkpoints. For nowaited checkpoints, the CHECKPOINT-BUSY-TIME counter includes the elapsed time to initiate nowaited checkpoint requests and the elapsed time for which the process is suspended in AWAITIO[X] waiting for the nowaited checkpoints to be completed. This counter is valid and displayed by MEASCOM in ZMS style when the PROCESS subsystem version (ZMSPROC.HDR.SUBSYSTEM- VERSION), supplied by Nonstop OS is eight or greater. Otherwise, the counter contains a zero value and is not displayed by MEASCOM. For more information, see Usage Notes for L-series PROCESS entities. This counter is not instrumented or displayed for KLT processes. AWAITIO[X] can be any API within AWAITIO family of APIs or FILE_COMPLETE_ family of APIs. Counter type: Busy. # COMP-TRAPSCOMP-TRAPS Number of times a compatibility trap occurred during execution of this process. A compatibility trap is a data misalignment event, an unexpected transition to or from accelerated or unaccelerated code, or a relative segment 2 or 3 problem. See the EPTRACE Manual for further details. Counter type: Incrementing. # CPU-BUSY-TIMECPU-BUSY-TIME Time that the CPU spent executing the measured process: In D-series RVUs, this counter is not advanced for the sending process when LDONE queuing is in use. For measurements on systems running G05 and later RVUs, this counter includes any page-defaulting activity performed on behalf of this process. For pre-G05.00 G-series RVUs, all page-faulting is charged to the memory manager process $VIRTUAL. For TNS/R systems, CPU-BUSY-TIME is the sum of ACCEL‑BUSY‑TIME, TNS‑BUSY‑TIME, and TNSR‑BUSY‑TIME. For TNS/E systems, CPU-BUSY-TIME is the sum of ACCEL‑BUSY‑TIME, TNS‑BUSY‑TIME, and NATIVE‑BUSY‑TIME. A CPU-BUSY-TIME value is always displayed. However, the ACCEL‑BUSY‑TIME, TNS‑BUSY‑TIME, and NATIVE‑BUSY‑TIME counter values are only calculated and displayed if a PROCESSH measurement was active during the measurement. For details about the behavior of the ACCEL‑BUSY‑TIME, TNS‑BUSY‑TIME, and NATIVE‑BUSY‑TIME counters, see the descriptions of these counters. ``` NOTENOTE The CPU‑BUSY‑TIME counter is enhanced for more precise measurement of processor utilization. Measurements on systems running D-series and G-series RVUs might show slightly higher utilization than comparable measurements on systems running C-series RVUs. The difference appears mainly on systems that run at low utilization levels. ``` ``` Related CounterRelated Counter DescriptionDescription CPU CPU-BUSY-TIMESimilar, but is the busy time for the entire processor. PROCESS CPU-NUM Identifies the CPU executing the process ``` Counter type: Busy. # CURRENT-PRIORITY-ENDCURRENT-PRIORITY-END (ZMS Style only) In H06.25/J06.14 and later RVUs, the current execution process priority at the end of the measurement interval. See also Usage Notes for H-series and later PROCESS Entities. Counter type: Snapshot. # CURRENT-PRIORITY-STARTCURRENT-PRIORITY-START (ZMS Style only) In H06.25/J06.14 and later RVUs, the current execution priority at the start of the measurement interval. See also Usage Notes for H-series and later PROCESS Entities. Counter type: Snapshot. # DISPATCHESDISPATCHES Number of times the process was selected from the ready list and executed by the CPU identified by the CPU‑NUM field. You can expect one dispatch for each I/O operation, plus additional dispatches caused by the process being preempted by higher priority processes. Counter type: Incrementing. # EXT-SEGS-QTIMEEXT-SEGS-QTIME Time that extended segments were allocated to the process through the SEGMENT_ALLOCATE_ family of APIs. This counter includes both user and system calls to this procedure. The system does not use any SEGMENT_ALLOCATE_ family of APIs when allocating segments for process code and data stack segments. Counter type: Queue. # FILE-AWAITIO-ARTFILE-AWAITIO-ART (ZMS Style only) The average response time for each call to the AWAITIO and FILE_COMPLETE_ family of APIs. This counter is supported in Measure L03 and later PVUs. FILE-AWAITIO-QTIME is divided by FILE-AWAITIO-CALLS. Counter type: Average Response Time. # FILE-AWAITIO-CALLSFILE-AWAITIO-CALLS (ZMS Style only) Number of calls to the AWAITIO and FILE_COMPLETE_ family of APIs. This counter is supported in Measure L03 and later PVUs. Counter type: Incrementing. # FILE-AWAITIO-QTIMEFILE-AWAITIO-QTIME (ZMS Style only) Time spent in the AWAITIO and FILE_COMPLETE_ family of APIs. This counter is supported in Measure L03 and later PVUs. Counter type: Queue. # FILE-NOWAIT-COMPLETEDFILE-NOWAIT-COMPLETED (ZMS Style only) Number of nowait I/O completions processed. This counter is supported in Measure L03 and later PVUs. Counter type: Incrementing. # FILE-NOWAIT-INITIATEDFILE-NOWAIT-INITIATED (ZMS Style only) Number of nowait I/Os initiated. This counter is supported in Measure L03 and later PVUs. Counter type: Incrementing. # FILE-OPEN-CALLSFILE-OPEN-CALLS # FILE-OPEN-CALLSFILE-OPEN-CALLS Number of calls to the FILE_OPEN_ or OPEN procedures. Counts all open calls including opens of regular files, pipes, FIFOs, AF_INET sockets, AF_UNIX sockets, directories, /dev/null, and TTYs. Includes implicit opens due to the fork(), exec*() family of process creation APIs. Counter type: Incrementing. # FILE-READSFILE-READS (ZMS Style only) Number of read operations. This counter is supported in Measure L03 and later PVUs. Counter type: Incrementing. # FILE-WRITESFILE-WRITES (ZMS Style only) Number of write, update and delete operations. This counter is supported in Measure L03 and later PVUs. Counter type: Incrementing. # GLOBALS-SIZEGLOBALS-SIZE (ZMS style only, L02, J06 and later) Size of the Globals. Counter type: Snapshot. # HEAP32-LIMITHEAP32-LIMIT (ZMS style only, L02, J06 and later) Maximum size allowed for the Heap. Counter type: Snapshot. # HEAP32-SIZE-ENDHEAP32-SIZE-END (ZMS style only, L02, J06 and later) Size of the Heap at the end of the measurement interval. Counter type: Snapshot. # HEAP32-SIZE-STARTHEAP32-SIZE-START (ZMS style only, L02, J06 and later) Size of the Heap at the start of the measurement interval. This counter is not displayed by MEASCOM but is available for IF/BY clauses and for plotting. Counter type: Snapshot. # HEAP64-LIMITHEAP64-LIMIT (ZMS style only, L02, J06 and later) Maximum size allowed for the Heap for a 64 bit process. Counter type: Snapshot. # HEAP64-SIZE-ENDHEAP64-SIZE-END (ZMS style only, L02, J06 and later) Size of the Heap for a 64 bit process at the end of the measurement interval. Counter type: Snapshot. # HEAP64-SIZE-STARTHEAP64-SIZE-START (ZMS style only, L02, J06 and later) Size of the Heap for a 64 bit process at the start of the measurement interval. This counter is not displayed by MEASCOM but is available for IF/BY clauses and for plotting. Counter type: Snapshot. # INFO-CALLSINFO-CALLS Number of calls to these procedures that resulted in a message being sent to a process (IOP or other) to obtain the information requested: ``` FILEINFO DEVICEINFO2 getsockopt() FILERECINFO access() pathconf() FILE_GETINFO_ fpathconf() select() FILE_GETINFOBYNAME_ fstat() sockatmark() FILE_GETINFOLISTBYNAME_ fstatvfs() stat() FILEGETINFO_LIST getpeername() statvfs() DEVICEINFO getsockname() ``` Counter type: Incrementing. # INITIAL-PRIORITY-ENDINITIAL-PRIORITY-END (ZMS Style only) In H06.25/J06.14 and later RVUs, the target (requested) process priority in effect at the end of the measurement interval. The initial priority specified for a process at the start of a measurement can be changed during execution by using the ALTPRI TACL command, or the ALTERPRIORITY, PRIORITY or PROCESS_SETINFO_ Guardian procedures. See also Usage Notes for H-series and later PROCESS Entities. Counter type: Snapshot. # INITIAL-PRIORITY-STARTINITIAL-PRIORITY-START (ZMS Style only) In H06.25/J06.14 and later RVUs, the target (requested) process priority in effect at the start of the measurement interval. The initial priority specified for a process at the start of a measurement can be changed during execution by using the ALTPRI TACL command, or the ALTERPRIORITY, PRIORITY or PROCESS_SETINFO_ Guardian procedures. See also Usage Notes for H-series and later PROCESS Entities. Counter type: Snapshot. # INTERRUPTSINTERRUPTS (ZMS style only, L01,H07/J05 and later) Total number of interrupts this process handled. This is a derived counter, which is the sum of the X-INTERRUPTS and Y-INTERRUPTS counters. Counter type: Incrementing. # IPU-AFFINITY-CLASS-ENDIPU-AFFINITY-CLASS-END (ZMS style only, L02, J06 and later) IPU affinity class of the process at the end of the measurement interval. Counter type: Snapshot. # IPU-AFFINITY-CLASS-STARTIPU-AFFINITY-CLASS-START (ZMS style only, L02, J06 and later) IPU affinity class of the process at the start of the measurement interval. Counter type: Snapshot. # IPU-NUMIPU-NUM (ZMS Style only) In Measure H05/J03 and later PVUs, the number of the IPU on which the process was executing when the sample was taken. On a non-NSMA system, this field is always 0 (zero). Counter type: Snapshot. # IPU-NUM-PREVIPU-NUM-PREV (ZMS Style only) In Measure H05/J03 and later PVUs, if an IPU switch took place during the measurement interval, IPU-NUM-PREV displays number of the IPU on which the process was executing before the most recent switch. If no IPU switch occurred during the measurement interval, this field is not displayed. On a non-NSMA system, this field is always 0 (zero). Counter type: Snapshot. # IPU-SWITCHESIPU-SWITCHES (ZMS Style only) Accumulating counter that indicates the total number of times that a process switched IPUs while executing. On a non- NSMA system, this field is always 0 (zero). In J06.07 and earlier RVUs, only DP2 processes showed valid values, while for all other processes the field was reported as 0 (zero). In J06.08 and later RVUs, the field contains valid values for all processes. Counter type: Accumulating. # LAUNCH-WAIT-TIMELAUNCH-WAIT-TIME In earlier PVUs, the amount of time the process waits on requests to PROCESS_CREATE_ or the fork() or exec*() process creation APIs. In Measure L03 and later PVUs, LAUNCH-WAIT-TIME is zero and not displayed by MEASCOM. Counter type: Busy. # LAUNCHESLAUNCHES Number of calls made by the process to process creation procedures. For Guardian process creation, the counter includes unsuccessful creation attempts. In L03 and later PVUs, replaced by PROCESS-LAUNCHES and not displayed by MEASCOM. Counter type: Incrementing. # LOCKED-PAGES-ENDLOCKED-PAGES-END (ZMS Style only) In Measure H06/J04 and later PVUs, the number of page frames associated with the process that were locked in main memory at the end of the measurement interval. This counter is valid and displayed by MEASCOM in ZMS style when the PROCESS subsystem version (ZMSPROC.HDR.SUBSYSTEM- VERSION), supplied by Nonstop OS, is four or greater; otherwise the counter contains a zero value and is not displayed by MEASCOM. Counter type: Snapshot. # LOCKED-PAGES-STARTLOCKED-PAGES-START (ZMS Style only) In Measure H06/J04 and later PVUs, the number of page frames associated with the process that were locked in main memory at the start of the measurement interval. This counter is valid and displayed by MEASCOM in ZMS style when the PROCESS subsystem version (ZMSPROC.HDR.SUBSYSTEM- VERSION), supplied by Nonstop OS, is four or greater; otherwise the counter contains a zero value and is not displayed by MEASCOM. Counter type: Snapshot. # MAINSTACK-LIMIT-ENDMAINSTACK-LIMIT-END # MAINSTACK-LIMIT-ENDMAINSTACK-LIMIT-END (ZMS style only, L02, J06 and later) Maximum size allowed for the Main stack at the end of the measurement interval. Counter type: Snapshot. # MAINSTACK-LIMIT-STARTMAINSTACK-LIMIT-START (ZMS style only, L02, J06 and later) Maximum size allowed for the Main stack at the start of the measurement interval. Counter type: Snapshot. # MAINSTACK-SIZE-ENDMAINSTACK-SIZE-END (ZMS style only, L02, J06 and later) Size of the Main stack at the end of the measurement interval. Counter type: Snapshot. # MAINSTACK-SIZE-STARTMAINSTACK-SIZE-START (ZMS style only, L02, J06 and later) Size of the Main stack at the start of the measurement interval. Counter type: Snapshot. # MEM-QTIMEMEM-QTIME Time that the measured process spent waiting on page faults. A page fault does not cause the system to remove the process from the ready list. ``` Related CounterRelated Counter DescriptionDescription PROCESS PAGE- FAULTS ``` ``` Counts the number of page faults generated by the measured process ``` ``` CPU MEM-QTIME Measures the time that all processes in a given processor spent waiting on page faults CPU CPU-QTIME Measures the time that all processes spent on the ready list, including time spent waiting on page faults ``` Counter type: Queue. # MESSAGES-RECEIVEDMESSAGES-RECEIVED Number of messages the process has read from its message input queue. For user processes, the message input queue is $RECEIVE. These operations do not advance this counter: Messages received by a process due to SIGNALTIMEOUT and SIGNALPROCESSTIMEOUT The sending process when LDONE queuing is in use Timeout notifications (TLEs) Counter type: Incrementing. # MESSAGES-SENTMESSAGES-SENT Number of messages sent by the linker process. User I/O operations (except on $RECEIVE) and calls to some system procedures increment this counter. Counter type: Incrementing. # MQC-ALLOC-FAILURESMQC-ALLOC-FAILURES Number of MQC requests that failed. Counter type: Incrementing. # MQC-ALLOCATIONSMQC-ALLOCATIONS Number of MQCs currently allocated to the process. Counter type: Incrementing. # MQCS-INUSE-QTIMEMQCS-INUSE-QTIME Time for which MQCs have been allocated to the process. Counter type: Queue. # MSGS-SENT-QTIMEMSGS-SENT-QTIME Time (in microseconds) that messages sent by the process were outstanding. Counter type: Queue. # NATIVE-BUSY-TIMENATIVE-BUSY-TIME (ZMS Style only) Time that the CPU was busy executing native code in this process. See Usage Notes for H-series and later PROCESS Entities. Counter type: Busy # OPEN-CLOSE-WAIT-TIMEOPEN-CLOSE-WAIT-TIME Amount of time the process waits on the open and close requests. For OSS processes, wait time accumulates for file open and close operations, excluding name server operations. Name server operations are counted in OSSNS-WAIT-TIME. Counter type: Busy. # OPEN-CLOSESOPEN-CLOSES (ZMS Style only) Number of OSS and Guardian open and close operations. This counter is supported in Measure L03 and later PVUs. Counter type: Incrementing. # OSS-DEV-NULL-OPSOSS-DEV-NULL-OPS Number of reads and writes to all files opened as /dev/null. Counter type: Incrementing. # OSS-TTY-READ-BYTESOSS-TTY-READ-BYTES Number of bytes read by the process from all files opened as /dev/tty. Counter type: Accumulating. # OSS-TTY-READSOSS-TTY-READS Number of READ operations performed by the process to all OSS file opens of /dev/tty. Counter type: Incrementing. # OSS-TTY-WAIT-TIMEOSS-TTY-WAIT-TIME Amount of time the process waits on requests to all files opened as /dev/tty. Counter type: Busy. # OSS-TTY-WRITE-BYTESOSS-TTY-WRITE-BYTES Number of bytes written by the process to all files opened as /dev/tty. Counter type: Accumulating. # OSS-TTY-WRITESOSS-TTY-WRITES Number of WRITE operations performed by the process to all OSS file opens of /dev/tty. Counter type: Incrementing. # OSSNS-ARTOSSNS-ART (ZMS Style only) Average response time for each OSS request. This counter is supported in Measure L03 and later PVUs. OSSNS-WAIT-TIME is divided by OSSNS-REQUESTS. Counter type: Average Response Time. # OSSNS-DD-CALLSOSSNS-DD-CALLS Number of data definition requests sent to OSS name servers. These APIs increment this counter: ``` bind() open(, O_CREAT) mkfifo() utime() chmod()link() rename() chown() mkdir() rmdir() creat() mknod() unlink() ``` Counter type: Incrementing. # OSSNS-MESSAGE-BYTESOSSNS-MESSAGE-BYTES Number of message data bytes sent and received for requests to OSS name servers. Counter type: Accumulating. # OSSNS-REDIRECTSOSSNS-REDIRECTS Number of redirected requests sent to OSS name servers. Counter type: Incrementing. # OSSNS-REQUESTSOSSNS-REQUESTS HPE Nonstop Measure Reference Manual 449 # OSSNS-REQUESTSOSSNS-REQUESTS Number of requests sent to OSS name servers. Requests can be fileinfo calls, data definition requests, directory operations, or resolution requests. Counter type: Incrementing. # OSSNS-WAIT-TIMEOSSNS-WAIT-TIME Amount of time the process waits on requests to all OSS name servers. Counter type: Busy. # PAGE-FAULTSPAGE-FAULTS Number of page faults generated by the process. Counter type: Incrementing. # PRES-PAGES-ENDPRES-PAGES-END Number of pages in main memory sponsored by the measured process at the end of the measurement interval. Counter type: Snapshot. # PRES-PAGES-QTIMEPRES-PAGES-QTIME In D-series RVUs, time that pages owned by the process spent in main memory. In G-series RVUs, the time that pages sponsored by the measured process spent in main memory. For a description of sponsoring, see Usage Notes for G-Series PROCESS Entities. Counter type: Queue. # PRES-PAGES-STARTPRES-PAGES-START Number of pages in main memory sponsored by the measured process at the start of the measurement interval. Counter type: Snapshot. # PRIVSTACK-LIMITPRIVSTACK-LIMIT (ZMS style only, L02, J06 and later) Maximum size allowed for the Priv stack. Counter type: Snapshot. # PRIVSTACK-SIZE-ENDPRIVSTACK-SIZE-END (ZMS style only, L02, J06 and later) Size of the Priv stack at the end of the measurement interval. Counter type: Snapshot. # PRIVSTACK-SIZE-STARTPRIVSTACK-SIZE-START (ZMS style only, L02, J06 and later) Size of the Priv stack at the start of the measurement interval. This counter is not displayed by MEASCOM but is available for IF/BY clauses and for plotting. Counter type: Snapshot. # PROCESS-LAUNCH-ARTPROCESS-LAUNCH-ART (ZMS Style only) Average time for each process launch. This counter is supported in Measure L03 and later PVUs. PROCESS-LAUNCH-QTIME is divided by PROCESS-LAUNCHES. This counter is valid when subsystem version is nine or greater. Otherwise, the counter contains a zero value and is not displayed by MEASCOM. Counter type: Average Response Time. # PROCESS-LAUNCHESPROCESS-LAUNCHES (ZMS Style only) Number of process launches. This counter is supported in Measure L03 and later PVUs. This counter is valid when subsystem version is nine or greater. Otherwise, the counter contains a zero value and is not displayed by MEASCOM. Counter type: Incrementing. # PROCESS-LAUNCH-QTIMEPROCESS-LAUNCH-QTIME (ZMS Style only) Time to launch processes. This counter is supported in Measure L03 and later PVUs. This counter is valid when subsystem version is nine or greater. Otherwise, the counter contains a zero value and is not displayed by MEASCOM. Counter type: Queue. # READY-TIMEREADY-TIME The time that the measured process spent on the ready list or executing. To keep the measurement overhead to a minimum, this counter is not turned off during certain system activities. This counter includes: Time required to dispatch the process. Time spent in software interrupts that are invoked asynchronously to the executing process. Time spent waiting for a page fault to be satisfied by the memory manager. (D-series) Time spent in message system routines. (D-series) Time spent in low-level message system routines when the process receives data or a message and the data transfer is not complete. The process is then suspended without being taken off the ready list timer. For example, a process might remain in the ready timer, not executing, while waiting for completion of a transfer of ServerNet or FOX data. (G-series) Due to changes in memory handling, the results of the READY-TIME counter for G05 and later RVUs are not directly comparable to earlier measurements. Previously, the ready list timer for a process remained active while waiting for a page fault even if the memory manager subsequently stalled waiting on I/O for the needed page. As of G05, each process handles its own page fault requests. If a request stalls while waiting for an I/O completion for the needed page, the ready timer is turned off. Counter type: Busy. ``` NOTENOTE Measure data can exhibit a combination of high PROCESS.READY-TIME and CPU.CPU-QTIME while having low PROCESS.CPU-BUSY-TIME and low overall processor utilization. Prior to G05, this was due to the suspension of PROCESS.CPU-BUSY-TIME while waiting for completion of a page fault. However, in all RVUs, a process can also be suspended while waiting for incomplete message system transfers to attempt completion. In either case, the PROCESS.READY-TIME and CPU.CPU-QTIME counters continue to accumulate. Although this message system behavior is rare, its occurrence might signal congested FOX or ServerNet/FX networks, possibly the result of paths in the network being marked as down, underlying hardware problems in the network, or data overload. ``` # RECEIVED-BYTESRECEIVED-BYTES Number of message bytes received by the listener process. The message bytes are also included in the SENT‑BYTES counter of the linker process that sent the message. In G-series and earlier RVUs, this is a 32-bit counter. In H-series and later RVUs, this is a 64-bit counter. The G-series RECEIVED‑BYTES‑F field is a 64‑bit version of RECEIVED‑BYTES. Counter type: Accumulating. # RECEIVED-CBYTESRECEIVED-CBYTES Number of protocol control bytes received by the message system on behalf of this process. This counter is updated when the process is acting as a listener process. The protocol control bytes are not included in the counter RECEIVED-BYTES. Counter type: Accumulating. # RECV-QTIMERECV-QTIME Time that messages spent waiting on the process message input queue. For user processes, the message input queue is $RECEIVE. Counter type: Queue. # REPLY-BYTESREPLY-BYTES Number of message bytes sent by a listener process in reply to a message from a linker process. The message bytes are also included in the RETURNED‑BYTES counter of the linker process that received the message. In G-series and earlier RVUs, this is a 32-bit counter. In H-series and later RVUs, this is a 64-bit counter. The G-series REPLY‑BYTES‑F field is a 64‑bit version of REPLY‑BYTES. Counter type: Accumulating. # REPLY-CBYTESREPLY-CBYTES Number of protocol control bytes sent by the message system on behalf of this process in reply to a message from a linker process. This counter is updated when the process is acting as a listener process. The protocol control bytes are not included in the counter REPLY- BYTES. Counter type: Accumulating. # RETURNED-BYTESRETURNED-BYTES Number of message bytes received by the linker process. The message bytes are also included in the REPLY‑BYTES counter of the listener process that sent the message. This counter does not include bytes transferred by direct bulk I/O. For DBIO byte counts, see the measurements attributed to the linker process in the DBIO-READ-BYTES counter of the FILE entity type. In G-series and earlier RVUs, this is a 32-bit counter. In H-series and later RVUs, this is a 64-bit counter. The G-series RETURNED‑BYTES‑F field is a 64‑bit version of RETURNED‑BYTES. Counter type: Accumulating. # RETURNED-CBYTESRETURNED-CBYTES Number of protocol control bytes returned by the message system on behalf of this process.
