EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. ``` (ERR^BADREPLY) ``` ``` (ERR^NEWDESCFIELD) ``` ``` (ERR^WRONGSYSTEM) ``` ``` (ERR^REMOTEDFILE) ``` RecoveryRecovery Because write access is not allowed for remote data files, use a data file on the same system as the calling process. ## 32103210 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The data file access number (DFNUM) parameter value did not match a valid DFNUM returned by a successful MEASOPEN call. EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. RecoveryRecovery Check that the value used is the same as the value returned by a MEASOPEN procedure. ## 32113211 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The measurement number (MEASNUM) parameter value did not match a valid MEASNUM returned by a successful MEASCONFIGURE call. EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. RecoveryRecovery Check that the value used is the same as the value returned by the MEASCONFIGURE procedure. ## 32123212 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause Measurement was not configured and active. EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. RecoveryRecovery Check that the measurement is configured and started and that it did not stop during the operation. ## 32133213 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause A CPU was up, but there was no MEASCTL to allocate and maintain counters in the CPU. EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. RecoveryRecovery ``` (ERR^BADDFNUM) ``` ``` (ERR^BADMEASNUM) ``` ``` (ERR^MEASNOTACTIVE) ``` ``` (ERR^NOMEASCTL) ``` Use the MEASMONCONTROL procedure, indicating the CPU number of the missing MEASCTL. ## 32143214 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause Insufficient physical memory is available. EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. RecoveryRecovery Redistribute your processes to other processors or install more memory. ## 32153215 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause A call to LINK failed. EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. RecoveryRecovery Retry the operation. ## 32163216 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause PROCESSH, DISCOPEN, and DISKFILE records are not available from the MEASREADACTIVE procedure. EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. RecoveryRecovery Call MEASREAD to obtain these records. ## 32173217 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The attempted call requires a super-group user ID (255,n). EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. RecoveryRecovery Have a super-group user execute the process that calls this procedure. ``` (ERR^MEMLOCKFAILURE) ``` ``` (ERR^LINK) ``` ``` (ERR^NOTAVAIL) ``` ``` (ERR^NOTSUPER) ``` ## 32183218 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The Measure subsystem was already started and cannot be started again. EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. RecoveryRecovery Verify that the Measure subsystem is not running prior to calling MEASMONCONTROL with a start subsystem request. ## 32193219 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The Measure subsystem was stopped. EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. RecoveryRecovery Verify that the Measure subsystem is running prior to calling this procedure. ## 32203220 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause A user-defined counter was not installed and cannot be bumped. EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. RecoveryRecovery Prior to this call, configure a measurement that includes the user-defined counters to be bumped. ## 32213221 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause An invalid BUMPTYPE was used for a user-defined counter. EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. RecoveryRecovery Check and correct the value of the BUMPTYPE parameter. ## 32223222 ``` (ERR^MONALREADYALIVE) ``` ``` (ERR^NOMEASMON) ``` ``` (ERR^UDCNOTPRESENT) ``` ``` (ERR^UDCBADBUMP) ``` ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause An invalid INDEX was used for a user-defined counter. EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. RecoveryRecovery Check and correct the value of the INDEX parameter. ## 32233223 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause A new or invalid entity type was supplied. EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. RecoveryRecovery Check that the entity type is valid and that it is supported in the RVU of the data file being processed. ## 32243224 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause MEASCTL internal consistency check failed. EffectEffect SQL activities are not measured. RecoveryRecovery Contact your Hewlett Packard Enterprise representative. ## 32253225 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The callable procedure was unable to complete a message to the MEASCTL process. EffectEffect The requested operation was ignored. RecoveryRecovery Try the operation again. If the operation continues to fail, contact your Hewlett Packard Enterprise representative. ## 32263226 ``` (ERR^UDCBADINDEX) ``` ``` (ERR^INVALIDENTITY) ``` ``` (ERR^INCONSISTENCY) ``` ``` (ERR^INCOMPLETEMSG) ``` ``` (ERR^LOCALSYSNAME) ``` ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The callable procedure cannot retrieve the caller’s local system name from Nodenumber_to_Nodename_. EffectEffect The requested operation was ignored. RecoveryRecovery Try the operation again. If the operation continues to fail, contact your Hewlett Packard Enterprise representative. ## 32273227 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause Measure encountered an internal error while working on the MEASFH file name. EffectEffect The requested operation aborted. RecoveryRecovery Check that the MEASFH object file exists and is properly secured. ## 32283228 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause An error was returned from Process_Create_. EffectEffect The requested operation aborted. RecoveryRecovery Check that the MEASFH object file exists and is properly secured. ## 32293229 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause An error was returned from File_Open_. EffectEffect Unable to open the MEASFH process. The requested operation aborted. RecoveryRecovery Check that the MEASFH object file exists and is properly secured. ## 32303230 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation ``` (ERR^MEASFH^INVALID^NAME) ``` ``` (ERR^MEASFH^CREATION) ``` ``` (ERR^MEASFH^OPEN) ``` ``` (ERR^MEASFH^SWAPFILE) ``` CauseCause The SWAPVOL value specified was in error. EffectEffect The MEASFH process or swap file is not created; the requested operation aborts. RecoveryRecovery Use a different SWAPVOL value or clear the SWAPVOL and use the default value. ## 32313231 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause You specified an unsupported entity type to the MEASLISTCONFIG procedure. The procedure supports only the DEVICE, DISC, FABRIC, LINE, NETLINE, OPDISK, and ENDPOINT (SERVERNET) entities. EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. RecoveryRecovery Issue the request again, specifying a supported entity type. ## 32323232 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The API was unable to allocate enough global buffer space from the flexible memory pool of the process. EffectEffect The operation is not performed. RecoveryRecovery Ensure that the application does not exhaust its flexible pool memory. ## 32333233 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The specified Guardian file name or OSS file pathname is no longer known to the OSS file system name server or does not match any data in the OSS journal segment. EffectEffect The command is not executed. RecoveryRecovery Ensure that the OSS file and directory names you specify in an entity specification are translatable at the time of the measurement. ## 32343234 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation ``` (ERR^CONFIG^NOT^AVAIL) ``` ``` (ERR^NOTENOUGH^PFSBUFFER) (ERR^NOTENOUGH^FPMEMORY) ``` ``` (ERR^INVALIDOSSPATH) ``` ``` (ERR^CRVSNNOTSPECIFIED) ``` CauseCause You called MEASLISTPNAME without specifying the CRVSN qualifier. EffectEffect Translation of the Guardian file name to an OSS file pathname is attempted, but the reported name might be incorrect if the file name specified is a transient entity. RecoveryRecovery Include the CRVSN value in the MEASLISTPNAME call. ## 32353235 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause MEASOPEN was called for a data file that was still in the process of OSS journal segment construction at measurement shutdown. EffectEffect The file is opened successfully, but the OSS journal segment is not attached. RecoveryRecovery Generally, no action is necessary. Translation can be processed from the OSS file system name space on the current system. If you need access to the OSS journal segment at a later time, the program must close and reopen the Measure data file after OSS journal segment construction is complete. You might notice that data access is slower for files opened during journal segment construction even after the journal segment construction step is completed. Closing and reopening the file improves the data-access speed. ## 32363236 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause A request was made that required an OSS journal segment for translation, but no OSS journal segment is attached in the current Measure data file context. EffectEffect The requested operation is aborted. RecoveryRecovery If you need translation from the OSS name server, omit the dfnum parameter or pass -1 as its value. If you need translation from a journal segment, ensure that one was collected with the data file and that error 3235 (ERR^BUILDINGOSSJOURNAL) was not returned on the MEASOPEN request. ## 32373237 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause A DISKFILE command contained an OSS directory name. EffectEffect The requested operation is aborted. RecoveryRecovery Only fully qualified OSS file pathnames are allowed in DISKFILE entity specification. If you need transient file activity or specific sets of files, use Guardian file naming. For example: $DATA.*.*, or $*.ZYQ00000.*. ``` (ERR^BUILDINGOSSJOURNAL) ``` ``` (ERR^MISSINGOSSJOURNAL) ``` ``` (ERR^OSSDIRNOTALLOWED) ``` ## 32383238 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The specified ANSI SQL name could not be translated, or the specified Guardian name could not be translated to an ANSI SQL name. EffectEffect The requested operation is aborted. RecoveryRecovery The name is no longer current in the ANSI SQL name space or is not present in the SQL journal. ## 32393239 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The specified ANSI SQL name could not be parsed due to syntax errors or was not fully qualified. EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. RecoveryRecovery In the entity specification syntax, check that the ANSI SQL name is syntactically correct and, if necessary, fully qualified. ## 32403240 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause MEASOPEN was called for a data file that was still in the process of SQL journal segment construction when the measurement shutdown. EffectEffect The data file opens successfully, but the SQL journal segment is not attached. RecoveryRecovery Generally, no action is necessary. Translations are processed through the SQL/MX services. If you later need access to the SQL journal segment, close and reopen the data file after SQL journal segment construction completes. ## 32413241 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause A request was made that required the SQL journal segment for translation, but no SQL journal segment is attached in the current Measure data file context. EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. RecoveryRecovery If you require translation from the ANSI SQL name server, omit the dfnum parameter or pass -1 as its value. If you require translation from a ``` (ERR^UNKNOWNSQLNAME) ``` ``` (ERR^BADFORMATSQLNAME) ``` ``` (ERR^BUILDINGSQLJOURNAL) ``` ``` (ERR^MISSING^SQLJOURNAL) ``` journal segment, ensure that you requested such a segment and that the MEASOPEN request did not return error 3240 (ERR^BUILDINGSQLJOURNAL). ## 32423242 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause A request was made for Measure functionality that does not exist in this version of Measure. EffectEffect The requested Measure function is not performed. RecoveryRecovery Upgrade your version of Measure to the required level for the specified function. ## 32433243 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause Disc space was not sufficient or segment allocation failed for building an OSS or ANSI SQL journal segment. EffectEffect File is opened successfully, but journal segment is not attached. RecoveryRecovery OSS name translation requests will be processed from the OSS name server on the current system. ANSI SQL names translation will be processed via SQL/MX on the current system. If access to the OSS or SQL journal segment is desired, then start a measurement on a disk having enough space. ## 32443244 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The application called a timercell function, but no timercell context is currently allocated. For example, the application called another timercell function without first invoking MEAS_ALLOCATE_TIMERCELLS_. Alternatively, all timercells have been deallocated, or the context was corrupt and error code 3245 (ERR^BAD^TIMERCELL^CONTEXT) was also returned. EffectEffect The requested operation is not performed. RecoveryRecovery Call MEAS_ALLOCATE_TIMERCELLS_ before invoking other timercell functions. ## 32453245 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The application called a timercell function, but the timercell context is corrupted. EffectEffect ``` (ERR^NOT^IMPLEMENTED) ``` ``` ERR^NOSPACEOSSJOURNAL ``` ``` (ERR^NO^TIMERCELL^CONTEXT) ``` ``` (ERR^BAD^TIMERCELL^CONTEXT) ``` The context is cleared, and all allocated timer cells for this process are lost. RecoveryRecovery Call MEAS_ALLOCATE_TIMERCELLS_ to create a new timercell context before invoking other timercell functions. ## 32463246 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The resource specified as the target of a callable function is not allocated. EffectEffect The operation is not performed. RecoveryRecovery Call the procedure that allocates the resource before you call any procedure that requires the resource. ## 32473247 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The application attempted to retrieve more than 128 timer cells, and because of insufficient system resources, Measure could not adjust the reported values of some counters to reflect the advance of time for repeated rendezvous operations. EffectEffect Data is returned for all timers, but timers in the penultimate group (modulo 128) have returned values slightly less than their true values. RecoveryRecovery Retrieve fewer than 128 timer cells at a time. ## 32483248 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The application attempted to allocate timer cells, but the timercell pool for the processor was insufficient to satisfy the request. EffectEffect The operation does not occur. RecoveryRecovery Use timer cells that have already been allocated. Alternatively, deallocate some existing timer cells, and allocate new ones. ## 32493249 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause A request was made for a resource or feature that is no longer supported. EffectEffect ``` (ERR^NOT^ALLOCATED) ``` ``` (WARN^TIMERCELL^ACCURACY) ``` ``` (ERR^NO^MORE^TIMERCELLS) ``` ``` (ERR^NOT^SUPPORTED) ``` The requested operation results in an error. RecoveryRecovery The only recovery from this error is to modify the application not to use that feature. Typically, support for a feature is removed when there is no reason to use that feature anymore and there are other better alternatives. Refer to the manual for further details. ## 32503250 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause A request for legacy style records was made for an entity that only supports ZMS style records. EffectEffect The requested operation results in an error. RecoveryRecovery Update the API call not to specify Legacy style records. The template version parameter must be specified with a nonzero value. ## 32513251 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The CONTAB format, which contains the entity descriptors to be measured, was incorrect. EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. RecoveryRecovery Correct any bad or missing CONTAB header, entity descriptor, or trailer record. Examine the CONTAB contents to the call. ## 32523252 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The data file did not have a file code of 175. EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. RecoveryRecovery Purge the file and create a new file with a file code of 175. ## 32533253 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The data file was not a disk file. EffectEffect ``` (ERR^NO^LEGACY^STYLE) ``` ``` (ERR^BADDESC) ``` ``` (ERR^FILECODE) ``` ``` (ERR^NOTDISCFILE) ``` The requested operation aborts. RecoveryRecovery Copy the measurement data file from tape to disk prior to read access. ## 32543254 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The MEASFH version did not match the data file version. EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. RecoveryRecovery Specify the MEASFH program file name from the RVU used to generate the data file. ## 32553255 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause An attempt was made to start a measurement on a measurement data file that was already open. EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. RecoveryRecovery Close the file and retry the operation. ## 32563256 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The MEASFH process received an error while attempting to sort a data file index segment. EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. RecoveryRecovery Close the file and retry the operation. ## 32573257 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The datafile record address array segment overflowed. EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. ``` (ERR^WRONGVERSION) ``` ``` (ERR^FILEINUSE) ``` ``` (ERR^ISEGOVERFLOW) ``` ``` (ERR^RSEGOVERFLOW) ``` RecoveryRecovery Try reducing the number of items to be LISTed by requesting specific items rather than using the asterisk (*) to list all items. For the PROCESSH entity, try specifying if code-range > 0 in the LIST command. ## 32583258 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause MEASFH's external counter record segment overflowed. EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. RecoveryRecovery Try reducing the number of items to be LISTed by requesting specific items rather than using the asterisk (*) to list all items. For the PROCESSH entity, try specifying if code-range > 0 in the LIST command. ## 32593259 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The measurement was not complete. EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. RecoveryRecovery Stop the measurement and retry the operation. ## 32603260 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The data file was not open. EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. RecoveryRecovery Call MEASOPEN for the data file and retry the operation that failed. ## 32613261 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The time interval was too small. EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. ``` (ERR^CSEGOVERFLOW) ``` ``` (ERR^MEASNOTDONE) ``` ``` (ERR^NOOPEN) ``` ``` (ERR^INTTOOSMALL) ``` RecoveryRecovery Retry the operation with a time interval equal to or greater than one second. ## 32623262 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause A counter record for the requested entity could not be found. EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. RecoveryRecovery Inspect the measurement CONTAB parameter containing the entity descriptors to be measured. Check for an entity descriptor that includes the current descriptor and verify that this entity was alive during the measurement. ## 32633263 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The PROCESSH code-range file was not a disk file. EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. RecoveryRecovery Move the code-range file to disk. ## 32643264 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The PROCESSH code-range file was not an object or edit file. EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. RecoveryRecovery Move the code ranges to a file in edit format, or use an object-format file to specify the code ranges. ## 32653265 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause A PROCESSH code-range file open or read error occurred. EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. ``` (ERR^NOCTRREC) ``` ``` (ERR^CRFILETYPE) ``` ``` (ERR^CRFILECODE) ``` ``` (ERR^CRFILE) ``` RecoveryRecovery Check the file name and verify that the file can be read (FUP COPY). Make any needed changes and retry the operation. ## 32663266 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause There was an error in the format of the PROCESSH code-range file. EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. RecoveryRecovery Correct the format and retry the operation. ## 32673267 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The PROCESSH code-range file did not specify any code ranges, or the existing code ranges do not occur in the specified code space. EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. RecoveryRecovery Add at least one code range to the file or correct the code space specification and retry the operation. ## 32683268 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The PROCESSH code-range file specified more than 500 code ranges. EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. RecoveryRecovery Delete enough code ranges from the file so that there are 500 or fewer and then retry the operation. ## 32693269 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The PROCESSH code-range table overflowed. Either the data file has been corrupted, or there is a problem with MEASFH. EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. RecoveryRecovery ``` (ERR^CRFMT) ``` ``` (ERR^NOCRS) ``` ``` (ERR^TOOMANYCRS) ``` ``` (ERR^CRTABLES) ``` If the data file was modified after the measurement was done, repeat the measurement and then retry the operation. Otherwise, retain the data file and contact your Hewlett Packard Enterprise representative. ## 32703270 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause Read access was requested for a data file that contains no data. EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. RecoveryRecovery Use another data file with measurement data in it or start a measurement using this file. ## 32713271 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause Too many PROCESSH code ranges are configured for measurement, and the CONTAB parameter is full. EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. RecoveryRecovery Reconfigure the PROCESSH measurement by reducing the number of code ranges (or taking out some code spaces). ## 32723272 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause An invalid request was made on a tape data file. EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. RecoveryRecovery The request can only be made on a disk file. Specify a disk file and then retry the operation. ## 32733273 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The MEASFH process has trapped out. EffectEffect The MEASFH process stops abruptly, and a saveabend file is created. RecoveryRecovery ``` (ERR^EMPTYDATAFILE) ``` ``` (ERR^CONTABFULL) ``` ``` (ERR^INVALID^TAPE^OP) ``` ``` (ERR^TRAP^OCCURRED) ``` If the trap number is 12 (%14), check the disk where the MEASFH work files were created. If the disk is full or very fragmented, use the MEASCOM SWAPVOL command or the SWAP parameter in the TACL RUN command to request that the MEASFH work files be created on another disk. If the trap is not caused by the inability to create an extent, the CPU is short of memory. In that case, try running the MEASFH process in a CPU with more memory. For traps other than trap 12, copy the displayed information and report the problem to your service provider. ## 32743274 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The MEASFH version is incompatible. The MEASFH process received a request it could not understand. Either the request is not implemented in the current MEASFH PVU, or the request is of an invalid type. EffectEffect The MEASFH process ignored the request. RecoveryRecovery If the request is valid, use a MEASFH file that supports the new request. Otherwise, supply a valid request. ## 32753275 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause An incompatible MEASFH version was specified. EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. RecoveryRecovery Specify the correct MEASFH version and retry the command. ## 32763276 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause Either the measurement was not started correctly on this data file (and error 3410 (ERRM^INSTALL) was returned by MEASMON), or START time was not written to the data file. EffectEffect The data file does not contain valid data. Further operations on the data file could result in error. RecoveryRecovery Try to start another measurement. Check the EMS log for error 3410 (ERRM^INSTALL). Use a data file big enough that the START time can be written to the data file. ## 32773277 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause MEASFH encountered an invalid data record when adding the data file. ``` (ERR^UNKNOWNMSG) ``` ``` (ERR^WRONGRELEASE) ``` ``` (ERR^MEAS^NEVER^STARTED) ``` ``` (ERR^DATAFILE^CORRUPT) ``` EffectEffect The data file is analyzed only up to the invalid record. RecoveryRecovery None possible. MEASFH cannot analyze the data file past the corrupted record. ## 32783278 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The MEASREADCONF call is an earlier PVU than MEASFH. EffectEffect MEASREADCONF could not handle an incomplete CONTAB parameter from MEASFH. It cannot read large measurement configurations. RecoveryRecovery Use a C20 or later PVU of the MEASREADCONF call. ## 32793279 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause MEASFH was unable to convert a TNS address to a TNS/R address while processing the EDIT file for SC or SL. EffectEffect The measurement is not configured. RecoveryRecovery Verify that each TNS address in the EDIT file is valid for the code space being configured. To do so, check the CONFLIST in SYSTEM.SYS nn. Although Measure accepts any number from 0 through %177777 for a TNS address, if the code space was accelerated, only addresses mapped by the Nonstop operating system into a TNS/R address are accepted in the EDIT file. ## 32803280 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The record size is different from the size in the data file. EffectEffect No records were written to the structured file. RecoveryRecovery Remove the existing structured file and let MEASFH create it, or use a different subvolume. If using MEASCOM, use the VOLUME command. Be sure to regenerate the proper DDL dictionary. ## 32813281 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause ``` (ERR^CONTAB^NOT^COMPLETED) ``` ``` (ERR^CONVERTING^TO^RISC) ``` ``` (ERR^FH^OLD^STRUCT^FILE) ``` ``` (ERR^FH^CTR^NAME^NOT^FOUND) ``` The counter name passed in the IF^ITEM structure was not found. Either the name is misspelled or it does not exist in the PVU being processed. EffectEffect The operation was not performed. RecoveryRecovery Pass a valid counter name. ## 32823282 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The counter name in the IF^ITEM structure is valid but cannot be used for the IF item. EffectEffect The operation was not performed. RecoveryRecovery Pass a different counter name. ## 32833283 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The MEASFH process was not able to obtain the extended segment size needed to perform the requested operation. EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. RecoveryRecovery Designate a CPU with more available memory and retry the command. ## 32843284 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The MEASFH process was not able to obtain the extended segment size needed to perform the requested operation. EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. RecoveryRecovery Designate a CPU with more available memory and retry the command. ## 32853285 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause ``` (ERR^FH^BY^CTR^ONLY) ``` ``` (ERR^RESIZESEGMENT) ``` ``` (ERR^ALLOCATE^SEG) ``` ``` (ERR^NOT^TNDM) ``` The MEASFH process was not able to read the COFF file further. EffectEffect The operation was not performed. RecoveryRecovery Designate a proper COFF file and retry the MEASCOM ADD PROCESSH command. ## 32863286 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The MEASFH process was not able to perform the requested operation because the native address in the edit file was not in ascending order. EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. RecoveryRecovery Make changes in the edit file and retry the command. ## 32873287 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause MEASFH was not able to perform the requested operation. The MEASMON process cannot obtain the memory space required to start this measurement. EffectEffect The operation was not performed. RecoveryRecovery Split the measurement into multiple measurements and retry the command. ## 32883288 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The MEASFH process was not able to read the COFF file further. EffectEffect The operation was not performed. RecoveryRecovery The COFF file is partially linked. Link the COFF file completely by passing it through the Native Mode Linker, NLD without the -r option, and then retry the operation with the output of the linker process. ## 32893289 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation ``` (ERR^EDIT^NOT^ASCEND) ``` ``` (ERR^NO^POOLSPACE) ``` ``` (ERR^NO^GINFO) ``` ``` (ERR^DATAFILE^INCOMPLETE) ``` CauseCause One or more CPUs ran out of CIDs, counter space, timercells, or all. Consequently, the data file is incomplete. EffectEffect The data file does not contain complete information for the measurement. RecoveryRecovery Retry the measurement with fewer entities specified. For more information, see the EMS log. ## 32903290 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The Measure PVU is not supported. EffectEffect The data file is not read. RecoveryRecovery Supply a valid Measure PVU and retry the operation. ## 32913291 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause MEASFH was not able to read the ELF file further. EffectEffect The operation was not performed. RecoveryRecovery The ELF file is partially linked. Pass the ELF file through the Native Mode Linker, NLD without the -r option, and then retry the operation with the output of the linker process. ## 32923292 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause MEASOPEN was called for a data file that was still in the process of journal segment construction at measurement shutdown, or MEASCONFIGURE was called with insufficient temporary file space for the MEASFH process to establish a journal segment. EffectEffect The data file is opened or the measurement is started, but without the OSS or ANSI SQL journal segment or name translation. RecoveryRecovery When adding a data file, use the MEASCLOSE and MEASOPEN calls to close and re-open the data file and attach the journal segment after construction is complete. When starting a measurement, verify adequate security for temp file creation and suitable space for the file (up to 64MB). ## 32933293 ``` (ERR^VERSION^NOT^SUPPORTED) ``` ``` (ERR^NO^EGINFO) ``` ``` (ERR^CANNOTMAKEJOURNAL) ``` ``` (ERR^NOT^REPORTFORMATNOTSTRUCTURED) ``` ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause LIST OSSNAMES or LIST EXTNAMES was issued without having set REPORT FORMAT STRUCTURED. EffectEffect No records are written to the OSSNAMES or EXTNAMES file. RecoveryRecovery Prior to issuing a LIST OSSNAME or LIST EXTNAMES command, specify REPORT FORMAT STRUCTURED. ## 32943294 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause Two files were specified for configuring the same code space range of the same object in a measurement configuration. MEASFH could not complete the measurement configuration because of the invalid specification. EffectEffect The operation was not performed. RecoveryRecovery Remove one of the two files from the specification. ## 32953295 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The MEASURE API internal check failed due to insufficient buffer space. EffectEffect The ANSI SQL name operation could not be performed. RecoveryRecovery Contact your Hewlett Packard Enterprise representative. ## 32963296 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause A call to the SQL/MX mapping API failed because no mapping process has been started. EffectEffect The ANSI SQL name operation could not be performed. RecoveryRecovery Verify that SQL/MX and TMF are installed and active. If that is the case, contact your Hewlett Packard Enterprise representative. ## 32973297 ``` (ERR^CONFIGFILECONFLICT) ``` ``` (ERR^SQL^API^INTERNAL) ``` ``` (ERR^SQLMX^MAP^PROCESS) ``` ``` (ERR^SQLMX^MAP^DUPCONNECTION) ``` ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause There is already an existing SQL/MX mapping session for this application process. EffectEffect No new mapping session is started. RecoveryRecovery If MEAS_SQL_MAP_STOP was just called, wait and try again, otherwise the message can probably be ignored and an already existing connectionInfo can be used to call the appropriate APIs. ## 32983298 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause A SQL/MX mapping session could not be started. EffectEffect No new mapping session is started. RecoveryRecovery Check if SQL/MX and TMF are installed and active. If that is the case, contact your Hewlett Packard Enterprise representative. ## 32993299 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The system time was changed during the measurement. Typically this error occurs at Daylight Savings Time transitions. EffectEffect Post processing results can be incorrect. RecoveryRecovery No recovery is possible. Run the measurement again. ## 33003300 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The type or length of the request was invalid. EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. RecoveryRecovery Correct the type or length and retry the request. ## 33013301 ``` (ERR^SQLMX^MAP^INIT) ``` ``` (ERR^LCT^CHANGE) ``` ``` (ERRM^INVALIDMSG) ``` ``` (ERRM^BADMEASNUM) ``` ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The measurement number supplied in the request was invalid for the requested service. EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. RecoveryRecovery Supply the correct measurement request number and retry the operation. ## 33023302 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The requester did not have proper security rights to perform the requested operation. EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. RecoveryRecovery Verify your request and retry the operation. ## 33033303 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The limit for the number of processes opening MEASMON has been exceeded. EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. RecoveryRecovery Wait for possible opener processes to end and then retry the operation. These openers could be any Measure applications, including MEASCOM. ## 33043304 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause An internal error occurred. EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. RecoveryRecovery Contact your Hewlett Packard Enterprise representative. ## 33053305 ``` (ERRM^SECURITYVIOLATION) ``` ``` (ERRM^OPENOVERFLOW) ``` ``` (ERRM^INTERNAL) ``` ``` (ERRM^DATAFILEINUSE) ``` ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause An attempt was made to use a data file that was already being used by another measurement. EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. RecoveryRecovery Specify another data file. ## 33063306 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause An attempt was made to configure more than 64 measurements. EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. RecoveryRecovery Wait until some measurements have stopped and retry the operation. ## 33073307 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The MEASMON CONTAB pool, containing entity descriptors, was full. EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. RecoveryRecovery Wait until some measurements have stopped and retry the measurement. ## 33083308 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The interval time is invalid. It cannot be less than one second or greater than one year. EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. RecoveryRecovery Correct the interval time value and retry the request. ## 33093309 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation ``` (ERRM^MEASTABLEFULL) ``` ``` (ERRM^NOCONTABSPAC) ``` ``` (ERRM^INVALIDINTERVALTIME) ``` ``` (ERRM^INVALIDSTARTSTOPTIME) ``` CauseCause In a control-measurement request, the stop time was less than the start time, an attempt was made to change the start time of an active measurement, or there was some other invalid start or stop time request. EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. RecoveryRecovery Correct the start and stop time values and retry the request. ## 33103310 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause An incorrect data file type was specified. EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. RecoveryRecovery Specify an unstructured disk file with a file code of 175. ## 33113311 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The CONTAB format was not correct. EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. RecoveryRecovery Correct your CONTAB format and retry the operation. ## 33123312 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause An attempt by MEASMON to open the data file failed. EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. RecoveryRecovery Check the current state of the data file and take appropriate action. ## 33133313 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation ``` (ERRM^INVALIDDATAFILE) ``` ``` (ERRM^INVALIDCONTAB) ``` ``` (ERRM^DATAFILEOPENFAILED) ``` ``` (ERRM^ADOPTFAILED) ``` CauseCause An attempt by MEASMON to adopt an existing MEASCTL or MEASIP process failed. EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. RecoveryRecovery Notify your system operator. ## 33143314 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The caller process has already opened MEASMON. EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. RecoveryRecovery The MEASMON process can be opened only once by the caller. It is already open. Check the MEASCB structure passed to the callable procedure. The caller should not have modified it. ## 33153315 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The backup for MEASMON did not start, one or more executing CPUs does not contain an active MEASCTL or MEASIP process, or both. EffectEffect The MEASURE subsystem is not fully operational. RecoveryRecovery Correct the problems indicated by the console messages. Then stop the MEASURE subsystem and restart it, ensuring that all MEASMON, MEASCTL, and MEASIP processes are stopped, before restarting the subsystem. ## 33163316 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The call to the LOCKMEMORY procedure failed. MEASMON was not able to perform the security check needed to access the data file. EffectEffect The measurement was not started. RecoveryRecovery Retry the request. If the problem persists, notify your system operator. ## 33173317 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation ``` (ERRM^ALREADYOPEN) ``` ``` (ERRM^START^MEASSUBSYS) ``` ``` (ERRM^MEMLOCKFAILURE) ``` ``` (ERRM^INVALIDBINADDR) ``` CauseCause An invalid code range was given in the PROCESSH entity. EffectEffect The measurement does not run. RecoveryRecovery Supply a valid code range address and retry. ## 33183318 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The PROCESSH configuration was not sent to MEASMON. MEASMON was unable to configure the measurement. EffectEffect The measurement was not started. RecoveryRecovery Retry the request. If the problem persists, contact your Hewlett Packard Enterprise representative. ## 33193319 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The pool space for code-range addresses is full in the MEASCTL process. EffectEffect The measurement does not run. RecoveryRecovery Stop some measurements and then try to start your measurement again. ## 33203320 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause An attempt by backup MEASMON to open the data file failed. EffectEffect The requested operation aborts. RecoveryRecovery Check the file system error number and take appropriate action. ## 33513351 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause ``` (ERRM^INVALIDBINPART) ``` ``` (ERRM^NOBINADDRSPACE) ``` ``` (ERRM^BACKUP^DATAFILEOPENFAILED) ``` ``` (ERR^OSSJOURNAL^INCOMPLETE) ``` OSS Journal Segment in MEASFH overflowed while expanding MIDs. EffectEffect Attached Journal segment does not contain all name translations. RecoveryRecovery Use fewer entities in the measurement configuration. ## 33523352 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause SQL Journal Segment in MEASFH overflowed while expanding MIDs. EffectEffect Attached Journal segment does not contain all name translations. RecoveryRecovery Use fewer entities in the measurement configuration. ## 34003400 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The complete request could not be read because the processor did not have sufficient physical memory. EffectEffect The reply file does not contain any data. RecoveryRecovery Install more memory. ## 34013401 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The requestor’s buffer was too small to complete the request. EffectEffect The reply file does not contain any data. RecoveryRecovery Allocate more space for the reply and retry the operation. ## 34023402 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The entity could not be accessed because of the current state. The CPU, group, module, slot number, SCSI ID, SAC name, lun, partition ``` (ERR^SQLJOURNAL^INCOMPLETE) ``` ``` (ERRM^READLINKFAILED) ``` ``` (ERRM^BUFFERTOOSMALL) ``` ``` (ERRM^CANNOTACCESS) ``` number, path, or CLIM name entered for the device may not be the correct value. Find out the correct value from SCF and retry the command. EffectEffect The reply does not contain any data. RecoveryRecovery If the device is inaccessible, contact your system operator. ## 34033403 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The entity descriptor in the request was invalid. EffectEffect The reply does not contain any data. RecoveryRecovery Correct the entity descriptor and retry the request. ## 34043404 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The measurement number (MEASNUM) parameter did not measure the entity, or the entity does not exist. EffectEffect The reply does not contain any data. RecoveryRecovery Check that the entity exists in the measurement CONTAB parameter. ## 34053405 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The reply did not fit in the MEASCTL reply buffer. This can result when there are too many user-defined counters to be returned in a reply to a READACTIVE request. EffectEffect The reply does not contain any data. RecoveryRecovery Eliminate some of the user-defined counters. ## 34063406 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause ``` (ERRM^ILLEGALDESC) ``` ``` (ERRM^NOTMEASURING) ``` ``` (ERRM^REPLYTOOBIG) ``` ``` (ERRM^ILLEGALREQ) ``` The request was invalid. EffectEffect The reply does not contain any data. RecoveryRecovery Check and correct the entity-descriptor type, check the actual request, and retry the operation. ## 34073407 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The measurement number (MEASNUM) in the request was not a valid measurement number, or there was no active measurement with this measurement number. EffectEffect The reply does not contain any data. RecoveryRecovery Supply the correct measurement number. ## 34083408 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause An attempt was made to start an already active measurement. EffectEffect The operation is aborted. RecoveryRecovery Contact your Hewlett Packard Enterprise representative. ## 34093409 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The MEASCTL CONTAB pool was full. EffectEffect The operation is aborted. RecoveryRecovery Wait until some other measurements have stopped and then retry the operation. ## 34103410 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause ``` (ERRM^ILLEGALMEASNUM) ``` ``` (ERRM^MEASNUMINUSE) ``` ``` (ERRM^NOCONTABSPACE) ``` ``` (ERRM^INSTALL) ``` A resource problem occurred with MEASCTL. EffectEffect The operation is aborted. RecoveryRecovery Wait until some other measurements have stopped or reduce the number of measurement entities, and then retry the operation. ## 34113411 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The data file could not be opened. EffectEffect The operation is aborted. RecoveryRecovery Check the current open state of the data file and its file security. Retry the operation with the right data file. ## 34123412 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The data file in use has reached EOF. EffectEffect Measurement is aborted. RecoveryRecovery Use a larger disk or use a larger data file. ## 34133413 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause Write to the current data file failed. EffectEffect Measurement is aborted. RecoveryRecovery Restart the current measurement. ## 34143414 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The call to the LOCKMEMORY procedure failed. ``` (ERRM^DFOPENFAILED) ``` ``` (ERRM^DFFULL) ``` ``` (ERRM^DFFAILED) ``` ``` (ERRM^LOCKMSGCTRLBUF) ``` EffectEffect Measurement is aborted. RecoveryRecovery Retry the request. If problem persists, notify your system operator. ## 34153415 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause SQL Control Block space is not available. EffectEffect Measurement is aborted. RecoveryRecovery Restart the SQL measurement. ## 34163416 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause Word length of the configuration record is extremely large. EffectEffect Measurement is aborted. RecoveryRecovery Restart the measurement. ## 34203420 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The MEASCTL process in the specified CPU ran out of internal counter space while starting a measurement. EffectEffect Some entities are not measured. RecoveryRecovery For all measurements in the system, reduce the amount of transient entities to be measured by configuring only needed entities. For example, avoid using wild-card (*) specifications in the configurations. Use the DELETE command to exclude entities that are not needed. ## 34213421 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The MEASCTL process in the specified CPU ran out of internal counter space while starting a measurement. ``` (ERRM^SQLSPACEOVERFLOW) ``` ``` (ERRM^INVALIDRECLEN) ``` ``` (ERRM^NOCIDENTRY) ``` ``` (ERRM^NOCOUNTERSPACE) ``` EffectEffect Some entities are not measured. RecoveryRecovery For all measurements in the system, reduce the amount of transient entities to be measured by configuring only needed entities. For example, avoid using wild-card (*) specifications in the configurations. Use the DELETE command to exclude entities that are not needed. ## 34303430 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause An illegal SQL statement index number was supplied in a MEASREADACTIVE or MEAS_READACTIVE_ call for an SQLSTMT entity. EffectEffect The operation is aborted. RecoveryRecovery Check that the number supplied is a legal statement index number for the desired SQL process. ## 34313431 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause Cannot access the entity because of its current state. EffectEffect Some SQLPROC, SQLSTMT, or OSSNS records will be missing in the datafile. RecoveryRecovery Reduce the scope of the specified measurement and retry the operation. ## 34323432 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause The descriptor is not found in contab. EffectEffect Some SQLPROC, SQLSTMT, or OSSNS records will be missing in the datafile. RecoveryRecovery Reduce the scope of the specified measurement and retry the operation. ## 34333433 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause Entity measurement is not active. ``` (ERRM^ILLEGALINDEX) ``` ``` (ERR^CANNOT^ACCESS) ``` ``` (ERR^DESCNOTFOUND^INCONTAB) ``` ``` (ERR^MEASUREMENT^NOTACTIVE) ``` EffectEffect Some SQLPROC, SQLSTMT or OSSNS records will be missing in the datafile. RecoveryRecovery Retry the operation. ## 34343434 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause Unable to get space in Buffer. EffectEffect Some SQLPROC, SQLSTMT or OSSNS records will be missing in the datafile. RecoveryRecovery Reduce the scope of the specified measurement and retry the operation. ## 34353435 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause SQL control block inconsistency during measurement. EffectEffect Some SQLPROC or SQLSTMT records will be missing in the datafile. RecoveryRecovery Contact your Hewlett Packard Enterprise representative. ## 34363436 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause SQL counter address inconsistency during measurement. EffectEffect Some SQLPROC or SQLSTMT records will be missing in the datafile. RecoveryRecovery Contact your Hewlett Packard Enterprise representative. ## 34373437 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause SQL structure inconsistency during measurement. EffectEffect ``` (ERR^GETPOOL) ``` ``` (ERR^SQLCB^INCONSISTENCY) ``` ``` (ERR^SQLCTR^INCONSISTENCY) ``` ``` (ERR^SQL^INCONSISTENCY) ``` Some SQLPROC or SQLSTMT records will be missing in the datafile. RecoveryRecovery Contact your Hewlett Packard Enterprise representative. ## 34403440 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause SQLSTMT space is full. EffectEffect Some SQLPROC or SQLSTMT records will be missing in the datafile, or an active request will return an empty external record. RecoveryRecovery Stop any SQLSTMT measurements to free up the space and retry the operation. ## 34413441 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause One or more CPUs ran out of SQL control block space, SQLSTMT map space, or both. EffectEffect Some SQLPROC or SQLSTMT entity records will be missing in the datafile. Consequently, the data file is incomplete. RecoveryRecovery Reduce the scope of the specified measurement and retry the operation. ## 34423442 ## Message ExplanationMessage Explanation CauseCause New threads are launched by a KLT process at a high rate. EffectEffect Unable to aggregate thread data for KLT process. Some process level records may be missing. RecoveryRecovery Use thread level records for the measurement. # Subsystem FilesSubsystem Files This list describes the files that make up the Measure performance monitor: ``` (ERRM^SQLSTMT^MAPPOOL^OVERFLOW) ``` ``` (ERRM^SQLSPACE^OVERFLOW) ``` ``` (ERRM^THREADS^EXCEEDED) ``` MEASCOM process MEASCOM is the Measure subsystem command interpreter. For descriptions of the commands, see MEASCOM Commands. Typically started from TACL, a MEASCOM process lets the user who started it carry on an interactive session with the Measure subsystem. A super-group user (member of user group 255) can use MEASCOM to start the Measure subsystem. Thereafter, any user can use MEASCOM to configure, run, and examine measurements. Concurrent sessions as well as concurrent measurements within a session are allowed. MEASCOM is stored in $SYSTEM.SYSnn. MEASFH process Created by the callable procedures whenever you run a measurement or later examine the resulting data file, one MEASFH process presides over one user‑designated data file: formatting it, adding data to it, and retrieving data as needed. MEASFH is release‑dependent in that a data file generated in one version can be examined only by using a MEASFH process of the same version. MEASFH is stored in $SYSTEM.SYSnn. In the H01 PVU, the default record style changed from Legacy to ZMS. Accordingly, in G-series and earlier RVUs, fields that appear only in ZMS style records and reports cannot be used in “IF” clauses, even in the ZMS style report mode. In H-series and later RVUs, fields that appear only in legacy style records and reports cannot be used in “IF” clauses, even in the legacy style report mode. MEASMON process Serving as the subsystem monitoring and coordinating process, a single MEASMON process (running as a process pair) is created when the Measure subsystem is started. MEASMON creates a MEASCTL process in each CPU and coordinates the activity of these processes, sending them measurement configuration requests and gathering information from them. MEASMON is stored in $SYSTEM.SYSnn. MEASCTL process MEASMON creates one MEASCTL process in each CPU when the Measure subsystem is started. The job of a MEASCTL process is to handle a description of counter space and to engage the relevant subsystems responsible for maintaining the counters. Among the tasks performed by MEASCTL are those that initialize counters when a measurement starts and those that copy counters to the data file. MEASCTL also retrieves for display the counters a user requests for online examination. MEASCTL is stored in $SYSTEM.SYSnn. MEASIP process MEASIP is the Measure Interrupt Process. It does the sampling for a PROCESSH measurement. The MEASMON process creates one or more MEASIP processes that run in each CPU of the local system when the Measure subsystem starts. This process is not present in RVUs prior to H-series and later. MEASIP is stored in $SYSTEM.SYSnnnn. MEASDDLS file The MEASDDLS file contains the record definitions (DDLs) of the optional structured files produced by the commands and procedures that display measurement results. You can use these record definitions to produce language-specific record declaration files for programmatic use of data or as the basis for user-developed reports using the Enform product. MEASDDLS is stored in $SYSTEM.SYSnn. MEASDDLF The MEASDDLF file contains FORTRAN DDL definitions. MEASDDLF is stored in $SYSTEM.SYSnn. MEASDDLB The MEASDDLB file contains COBOL85 DDL definitions. MEASDDLB is stored in $SYSTEM.SYSnn. MEASDDLZ file The MEASDDLZ file lets applications access ZMS style data records with minimal impact. The ZMS style record structure adds a level of naming to each component of the record. The legacy format does not have this additional naming level. For example, the field cpu.dispatches in a legacy style record is equivalent to the zmscpu.ctr.dispatches in the ZMS style record structure. Converting existing applications and ENFORM reports from the legacy naming format to the ZMS style naming format is a significant edit of record references. It also involves removal of fields that might be referenced by programs, and the possible combination of equivalent fields that appeared in two sizes (for example, process.sent-bytes and process.sent-bytes-f). To simplify this process, use the MEASDDLZ file. MEASDDLZ presents the ZMS style counter widths and locations in the record using the legacy style record naming convention. Record template names use legacy style names (for example, PROCESS rather than ZMSPROC). Fields that have been combined are referenced by redefines. MEASDECS file The MEASDECS file contains the structure declarations and literal value definitions used in the Measure callable procedures. MEASDECS is stored in $SYSTEM.SYSnn. See C and C++ Language Usage Notes. MEASCHMA file The MEASCHMA file contains the structure declarations for C and TAL. Its output is similar to the output of the MEASDECS file. See C and C++ Language Usage Notes. MEASIMMU file The MEASIMMU file contains the Measure error messages and the online help text accessible through the MEASCOM HELP command. MEASIMMU is stored in $SYSTEM.SYSnn. ##### NOTENOTE ``` MEASCTL, MEASFH, MEASIP, and MEASMON must be on the active system subvolume (SYSnn). The remaining Measure files need not be located on the active subvolume. ``` SubtopicsSubtopics C and C++ Language Usage NotesC and C++ Language Usage Notes Process Identification NumbersProcess Identification Numbers # C and C++ Language Usage NotesC and C++ Language Usage Notes To see whether a field is within a union, refer to the C declarations in files MEASCHMA and MEASDECS. If the field is within a union, include the union name when writing code that uses the field. Existing C or C++ programs that use Measure data might not compile using the C structure declarations in new PVUs of Measure’s MEASCHMA or MEASDECS. References to fields that now are within a union must be changed to include the union name in the reference. # Process Identification NumbersProcess Identification Numbers Process identification numbers (PINs) on systems running D‑series, G‑series, and later RVUs are divided into these two ranges: A low PIN range from 0 through 254 A high PIN range from 256 through the maximum number supported for the CPU The PIN 255 is used only for a synthetic process ID. For more information on PIN 255, see the Guardian Application Conversion Guide. Processes on C‑series RVUs run at low PINs. A process running at a high PIN cannot communicate with a process or a file that resides on a system running a C‑series RVU. To let a D‑series, G‑series, or later process access a C‑series process, you must run the D‑series, G-series, or later process at a low PIN. Table 1. PIN Information for the Measure Product ProcessProcess Can run at high PINCan run at high PIN Defaults to high PINDefaults to high PIN MEASCOM No N.A. MEASMONYes Yes MEASCTL Yes Yes MEASFH Yes, in Measure H02 and later PVUsYes, in Measure H02 and later PVUs When you run the Measure product on a system running a D‑series, G‑series, or later RVU, you need not be concerned about PIN ranges. However, to access a measurement data file on a system running a C‑series RVU, you must run the Measure product at a low PIN. To run a Measure process at a low PIN, use the /HIGHPIN OFF/ option in the TACL RUN command, or set the TACL HIGHPIN built‑in variable to OFF. ``` NOTENOTE To examine data files from D‑series or previous RVUs on systems running G‑series or later RVUs, you must use a Dnn PVU of MEASCOM as well as the appropriate MEASFH. For maximum compatibility with a variety of data files, always use the most recent Dnn PVU of MEASCOM. ``` # Measure Data File Tool (MEASFT)Measure Data File Tool (MEASFT) HPE Nonstop Measure Reference Manual 741 # Measure Data File Tool (MEASFT)Measure Data File Tool (MEASFT) The Measure Data File Tool (MEASFT) lets you split a measurement data file into multiple data files for consumption and display by MEASCOM or through Measure APIs. MEASFT is especially useful in the case of the very large data files that can result from measuring large numbers of entities or measuring at very small intervals. MEASFT is part of H06.07 and later RVUs, but you can use it to split measurement data files created by Measure G10 or later G-series PVUs. To split a G-series data file, you must first move the file to a system running an H-series and later RVU. After running MEASFT, either move the output data files back to the original system for processing and display, or process them on the system running an H-series and later RVU, using the MEASFH option of the ADD command to specify the appropriate MEASFH object file version. ``` NOTENOTE In Measure G12 or later G-series PVUs, it is no longer necessary to move a G-series data file to a system running an H-series and later RVU in order to split the file. ``` MEASFT has two major functions, each invoked by a command: SPLIT. This command splits one measurement data file into multiple measurement data files. You can specify whether to split the file by entity type(s), processor number, or based on a time window (in H06.25/J06.14 and later RVUs only). Measurement configuration, START/STOP measurement records, and any applicable OSS and SQL journal data are replicated in each output data file. INFO. This command reads a measurement data file and displays a report indicating how much file space is used for data associated with each entity type and processor number. This information can help you decide how best to split the data file. MEASFT also has a HELP command, which displays syntax requirements for MEASFT commands. SubtopicsSubtopics INFO CommandINFO Command SPLIT CommandSPLIT Command HELP CommandHELP Command MEASFT Error and Warning MessagesMEASFT Error and Warning Messages # INFO CommandINFO Command The INFO command produces a report that indicates how much space in a data file is associated with each entity type and processor type. You can use this information to help you determine the best split criteria for the file. In H06.25/J06.14 and later RVUs, the INFO command also displays a split history record of the data file. The split history information contains the time when the data file was created, the MEASFT banner of the MEASFT program that performed the split, and the command line that created the data file. In addition, the time period and interval values will be displayed, if relevant: ``` If the data file is a TIME-split file (a data file created by the SPLIT BY TIME command) or is a descendent of a TIME-split file, the INFO command also displays the time period value of the data file, which is a subset of the time period for the original data file. If the data file is for an interval measurement, or is a descendent of an interval measurement, the INFO command also displays the interval value. ``` In L-series, the INFO command will list the FABRIC entities usage if they are present in the data file. Configuration, START/STOP records, and journal data are listed separately and not included in the entity and processor usage statistics. SubtopicsSubtopics ``` SyntaxSyntax ExampleExample ``` # SyntaxSyntax filename identifies the Measure data file to be examined. Use a full or partially qualified Guardian file name. # ExampleExample ``` Measure data files created on systems running H07 Measure will display ENDPOINT instead of SERVERNET. ``` ``` In H06.25/J06.14 and later RVUs, the INFO command display includes a split history record of the data file. The following example is a display of a data file (MDFD00) that was the result of splitting MDFALLI by TIME into 1 hour PARTS, followed by a split of MDFA01 by CPU, followed by a split of MDFB01 by ENTITY, followed by a split by TIME to extract 15 minutes of data starting at 11:30. Notice that the SPLIT History entries for TIME-based splits display an extra line that shows the time period of the split file. ``` ``` INFO filename ``` ``` $MEAS1 KMAZAWA 5> measft info mdf5ea15 MEASURE File Tool - T9086L01 - (15NOV14) - \MEASCOG (C) Copyright 2007-2014 Hewlett-Packard Development Company, L.P. INFO MDF5EA15 \MEASCOG - Sys id: 77153 $MEAS1.KMAZAWA.MDF5EA15 (H07) File Size 4681768 bytes Data file usage (in bytes) by Entity Entity Space % ------ ---------- ------ CPU 59192 1.26 DISC 126816 2.71 ENDPOINT 104952 2.24 FABRIC 480520 10.26 PROCESS 3458360 73.87 ------ ---------- ------ Total 4229840 90.35 Data file usage (in bytes) by Processor Number CPU Space % ------ ---------- ------ 0 887592 18.96 1 792560 16.93 2 494632 10.57 3 499680 10.67 4 388944 8.31 5 388056 8.29 6 389280 8.31 7 389096 8.31 Data file usage for configuration is 451928 bytes (9.65%) Measurement period of the original data file Start time: February 6, 2014 19:47:30 Stop time: February 6, 2014 19:52:23 Interval: 15 seconds $MEAS1 KMAZAWA 6> ``` ``` $PERF.CAPAPP 156> MEASFT INFO MDFD00 MEASURE File Tool - T9086J04 - (01AUG12) - \BLPROD ``` In pre-H06.25/J06.14 RVUs, the INFO command display does not include the split history record of the data file: ``` (C) Copyright 2007-2012 Hewlett-Packard Development Company, L.P. INFO MDFD00 \BLPROD - Sys id: 000001 $PERF.CAPAPP.MDFD00 (H06) File Size 24619732 bytes Data file usage (in bytes) by Entity Entity Space % ------ ---------- ------ FILE 24617552 99.99 ------ ---------- ------ Total 24617552 99.99 Data file usage (in bytes) by Processor Number CPU Space % --- ---------- ------ 2 24617552 99.99 Data file usage for configuration is 2180 bytes (0.01%) Time period of this Split data file From: March 16, 2012 11:30:00 To: March 16, 2012 11:45:00 Measurement period of the original data file Start time: March 16, 2012 10:26:09 Stop time: March 16, 2012 14:32:34 Interval: 2 minutes SPLIT History Number of Splits is 4 Entry 1, Timestamp April 10, 2012 17:01:23 Banner: MEASURE File Tool - T9086J04 - (01AUG12) - \BLPROD CmdLin: SPLIT MDFALLI,MDFA*,BY TIME,INTO 1 HOUR PARTS Time period: 16 Mar 2012, 11:26:09 to 16 Mar 2012, 12:26:09 Entry 2, Timestamp April 10, 2012 17:04:13 Banner: MEASURE File Tool - T9086J04 - (01AUG12) - \BLPROD CmdLin: SPLIT MDFA01,MDFB*,BY CPU * Entry 3, Timestamp April 10, 2012 17:05:22 Banner: MEASURE File Tool - T9086J04 - (01AUG12) - \BLPROD CmdLin: SPLIT MDFB02,MDFC*,BY ENTITY * Entry 4, Timestamp April 10, 2012 17:07:22 Banner: MEASURE File Tool - T9086J04 - (01AUG12) - \BLPROD CmdLin: SPLIT MDFC04,MDFD*,BY TIME,FROM 11:30,TO 11:45 Time period: 16 Mar 2012, 11:30:00 to 16 Mar 2012, 11:45:00 ``` ``` $PERF.CAPAPP 28> MEASFT INFO MDATA5 MEASURE File Tool - T9086H01 - (01AUG06) - \YOSPRD Copyright 2006 Hewlett-Packard Company INFO MDATA5 $PERF.CAPAPP.MDATA5 (H01) File Size 133980160 bytes Data file usage (in bytes) by Entity ``` # SPLIT CommandSPLIT Command The SPLIT command splits the specified data file into multiple data files. It can also be used to produce a single file comprising selected records from the original data file. You can specify whether to split the file by entity type(s), processor number, or based on a time window (in H06.25/J06.14 and later RVUs only). After splitting the data file, MEASFT displays a summary of the resulting split, showing the output data file names, size and criteria. SubtopicsSubtopics SyntaxSyntax SPLIT Command Usage NotesSPLIT Command Usage Notes ExamplesExamples # SyntaxSyntax filename specifies the data file to be split. It can be a partially or fully qualified Guardian file name (that is, you can specify or omit the node, volume and subvolume). ``` Entity Space % ------ ---------- ------ CPU 133056 0.10 DISC 2031486 1.52 DISCOPEN 25678814 19.17 DISKFILE 17085880 12.75 FILE 35348390 26.38 NETLINE 173644 0.13 OSSCPU 155626 0.12 OSSNS 23394 0.02 PROCESS 41238946 30.78 SERVERNET 10585470 7.90 SQLPROC 9956 0.01 SQLSTMT 82328 0.06 TMF 29766 0.02 ------ ---------- ------ Total 132576756 98.95 Data file usage (in bytes) by Processor Number CPU Space % --- ---------- ------ 0 28409802 21.20 1 19602142 14.63 2 41878388 31.26 3 42686424 31.86 Start time: March 10, 2006 08:19:05 Stop time: March 10, 2006 08:38:53 ``` ``` SPLIT filename [, out-file-set [, split-criteria ] ] ``` out-file-set specifies the names of the files to be created from the split. Specify the set in one of these ways: If you omit both the out-file-set and the split-criteria, MEASFT divides the input data file into two files, named ZMSRF000 and ZMSRF001, using split criteria that make the split as even as possible. If you specify an asterisk (*), MEASFT creates the output data files with names of the form ZMSRF nnn, beginning with ZMSRF000. The number of output data files created depends upon split-criteria. If split-criteria is not specified, MEASFT behaves as described in SPLIT Command Usage Notes. If you specify a string of characters ending in an asterisk, MEASFT regards the value as a file name prefix and appends a number, starting with "00" for the first file. The number of output data files created depends upon the split-criteria. If split-criteria is not specified, MEASFT behaves as described in SPLIT Command Usage Notes. If you specify a comma-separated list of file names, enclosed in parentheses, MEASFT uses those names for the files it creates. You must specify at least one name. If the number of specified names is different from the number of output data files dictated by the split-criteria, MEASFT terminates with an error message. If you specify only one name, you must specify split-criteria to indicate what should go into the single output data file. name-prefix, namen and name are partially or fully qualified names. For example, all of the following specifications are valid: \node.$vol.subvol.* subvol.pref* (split1, \node.$vol.subvol.rsplit2, othersv.split3) If you specify an output file name that is the name of an existing Measure data file, but not the name of the data file to be split (that is, the specified output file name is not the file name identified by filename), MEASFT displays a warning message and purges the data before writing to the file. If you specify an output file name that is the same as the name of the existing Measure data file to be split, MEASFT displays an error message and terminates. If you specify an output file name that is the name of an existing file, but not a Measure data file, MEASFT displays an error message and terminates. split-criteria specifies how the data file will be split. Its syntax is one of: ``` BY ENTITY followed by an asterisk (*) causes MEASFT to create one output data file per entity type present in the data file. BY ENTITY followed by one or more entity lists causes MEASFT to create one output data file for each list of entities. The syntax for entity-listn is: entity-name [entity-name]... that is, a space-separated list of entity-name. The permissible values for entity-name are: CLUSTER CPU DEVICE ``` ``` * name-prefix * ( name1 [, name2 ]...) name ``` ``` BY ENTITY * BY ENTITY ( entity-list1 [, entity-list2 ]...) BY CPU * BY CPU ( cpu-list1 [, cpu-list2 ]...) ``` ``` BY TIME, FROM time [, { TO time | FOR duration } ] BY TIME, TO time BY TIME, FOR duration BY TIME, INTO number [ SECOND | MINUTE | HOUR ] [ PARTS ] ``` DISC or DISK DISCOPEN or DISKOPEN DISKFILE ENDPOINT FABRIC FILE LINE NETLINE OPDISK OSSCPU OSSNS PROCESS PROCESSH SERVERNET SQLPROC SQLSTMT SYSTEM TERMINAL TMF USERDEF The last entity-listn can also be an asterisk ( * ), which instructs MEASFT to put data for all the remaining entities into the last output data file. If only entity-list1 is specified, a single output data file is created. BY CPU followed by an asterisk (*) causes MEASFT to create one output data file per processor number present in the data file. BY CPU followed by one or more lists of processor numbers causes MEASFT to create one output data file for each list of processor numbers. The syntax for cpu-listn is: that is, a space-separated list of cpu-num. The last cpu-listn can also be an asterisk ( * ) which means to put all of the rest of the processor data into the last output data file. If only cpu-list1 is specified, a single output data file is created, to contain data for the processor numbers in the list. In H06.25/J06.14 and later RVUs only, you can also specify whether to split the file based on a BY TIME split criteria. BY TIME, FROM time causes MEASFT to create one output data file that contains entities that existed in the input data file from the time specified. BY TIME, FROM time can optionally be followed by either a TO time or a FOR duration specification. If TO time is specified, the TO time defines an end time to the time window. If FOR duration is specified, the FOR duration defines the duration of the time window. BY TIME, TO time causes MEASFT to create one output data file that contains entities that existed in the input data file from the measurement start time up to the time specified. BY TIME, FOR duration causes MEASFT to create one output data file that contains entities that existed in the input data file from the measurement start time for the duration indicated. ``` cpu-num [cpu-num] ... ``` ##### NOTENOTE ``` MEASFT supports Measure data file versions from G10 to H06. However, the BY TIME split criteria is only supported for data file versions H01 to H06 because the necessary time information needed by the SPLIT BY TIME command did not exist prior to H01. Only one output file (split file) is supported when the FROM, TO and FOR parameters are used with the BY TIME split criteria. ``` ``` The syntax for time is: ``` ``` where date is ``` ``` where dd is a day of the month, a number in the range 1 through 31. mmm is the first three letters of the month; for example: JAN, MAR, OCT. yyyy is a 4-digit year. If you omit date, the date that the measurement was started is used. time ‑ of ‑ day is ``` ``` where hh is the hour (0 through 23). mm is minutes (0 through 59). ss is seconds (0 through 59). The syntax for duration is one of: ``` where n is an integer in the range 1 through 32767. BY TIME, INTO number causes MEASFT to create multiple output data files that contains entities that existed in the input data file. BY TIME, INTO number can optionally be followed by a SECOND, MINUTE, HOUR, or PARTS specification. If BY TIME, INTO number is specified without a SECOND, MINUTE, HOUR, or PARTS specification or BY TIME, INTO number PARTS is specified, number indicates the number of split output data files into which the input file should be divided and can have a value of 2 to 100. If BY TIME, INTO number is followed by the SECOND keyword, number defines the number of seconds of input data that go into each split output data file. If BY TIME, INTO number is followed by the MINUTE keyword, number defines the number of minutes of input data that go into each split output data file. If number is followed by the HOUR keyword, number defines the number of hours of input data that go into each split output data file. When followed by SECOND, MINUTE, or HOUR, number can have a value of 1 to 32767. If a time unit is specified, the data in the last split output data file may have a shorter duration than the others. The maximum number of split output data files is 100. If the BY TIME, INTO specification dictates that more than 100 split output data files be created, only 100 split output data files will be created with the 100th split output data file containing the rest of the data records. For example, splitting a 2-hour measurement into 1-minute parts will result in the first 99 split output data files containing 1 minute of data, and the 100th data file containing 21 minutes of data. ``` [ date , ] time‑of‑day ``` ``` dd mmm yyyy ``` ``` hh : mm [: ss ] ``` ``` n SECOND[S] n MINUTE[S] n HOUR[S] ``` # SPLIT Command Usage NotesSPLIT Command Usage Notes ``` When the SPLIT BY TIME command is executed on an interval data file, it includes a tolerance factor in the beginning and ending times of the split file time period. This tolerance is similar to the default tolerance that MEASCOM employs when the FROM and TO options are used for the LIST command. For example, if the beginning and ending times for a split file are 5:00 and 6:00, respectively, and if the measurement interval is 10 minutes, MEASFT will actually use a beginning time of 4:55 and an ending time of 6:05 in deciding what data records to include in the split file. This tolerance was added so that Measure reports from the split file will be identical to the same report from the original file. However, on busy systems, the MEASCTL or DP2 processes may not be able to write to the measurement data file in a timely manner. In addition, there may be significant clock skew (relative to the measurement interval) between CPUs. These conditions can create “edge effects” that affect the fidelity between the original data file and split file MEASCOM LIST output. To ensure fidelity, the user should specify time period boundaries about one interval larger than the time period of interest. Using the previous example, to get reliable MEASCOM LIST output between 5:00 and 6:00, the user should specify a FROM time of 4:50 and a TO time of 6:10 (or equivalent options) in the MEASFT SPLIT BY TIME command when the split file is created. The “Time period” line of the SPLIT display reflects the time period covered by the input data file. For an original measurement data file, it’s the Start and Stop time of the measurement. For a TIME-split data file, or one that is a descendent of a TIME-split data file, the “Time period” line reflects the time period of the data file. The “Time Period” column of the SPLIT display shows the time period of each created TIME-split data file. All the data records in the file should have a timestamp within that period. For interval data files, the effective time period is wider by one half of the interval on either side of the displayed time period. The number in the “%” column of the SPLIT display is the size of the output data file relative to the input data file. When all the data records of the input data file are being split among a set of output data files, you might expect that the sum of the “%” numbers would add up to 100%, but this is rarely the case. The configuration records (including any journals) are copied into each split output data file. (In addition, if the input data file is an interval data file, data records with timestamps that are near the time period boundaries (see note on the “Time Period” column above) will appear in both adjacent split files.) If the input data file is an original data file (that is, one created by MEASCOM), the file will contain slack bytes in many of the data blocks. Because MEASFT eliminates slack bytes before it writes the data records to the split output data files, the sum of the “%” numbers may be more or less than 100%. When generating a report or plot for a data file that has been split using the SPLIT BY TIME command, you should specify FROM and TO options (within the time period of the TIME-split data file) in the MEASCOM LIST entity-type, LISTALL entity-type, or LIST PLOT command to ensure fidelity between Measure reports and plots done on the original data file with those done on the TIME-split data file. If you specify an asterisk (*) as the out-file-set and omit the split-criteria parameter, MEASFT chooses the number of output data files by dividing the Measure data file size by 1024 MB (1,073,741,824 bytes) and rounding down. It uses this number or 2, whichever is larger, as the number of output data files. It then groups the entities to enable as even a split as possible. For example: $PERF.CAPAPP 113> ``` ``` MEASURE File Tool - T9086H01 - (01AUG06) - \YOSPRD Copyright 2006 Hewlett-Packard Company SPLIT BIGDATA,* File $PERF.CAPAPP.BIGDATA was split into the following files: File Size % Entity(s) ----------------------------------- ---------- ------ --------------------- $PERF.CAPAPP.ZMSRF000 27391786 2.69 CPU,DISC,NETLINE, SYSTEM,TMF,SQLPROC, SQLSTMT,OSSCPU,OSSNS $PERF.CAPAPP.ZMSRF001 51509214 5.06 DISKFILE $PERF.CAPAPP.ZMSRF002 86759610 8.52 SERVERNET $PERF.CAPAPP.ZMSRF003 145165294 14.26 DISCOPEN $PERF.CAPAPP.ZMSRF004 165726444 16.28 FILE $PERF.CAPAPP.ZMSRF005 190483848 18.71 PROCESS $PERF.CAPAPP.ZMSRF006 337090908 33.11 PROCESSH ``` ``` MEASFT SPLIT BIGDATA,* ``` ``` If you specify an asterisk (*) after the BY TIME option, MEASFT displays error message 3262. ``` # ExamplesExamples ``` Extract the records from data file, MDFALLI, that occur during the 15 minutes after 16 Mar 2012, 11:15, and write them into a new data file, MIN15. The “Time period” line is the time period of the input data file being split. The “Time Period” column is the time period of each created time-split file. The number in the “%” column is the size of the output data file relative to the input data file. ``` ``` Split Measure data file, MDFALLI, into data files, named MDFAnn, which each have 1 hour of Measure data. In this case, the input data file contains just over 4 hours of data, so 5 output data files are created. ``` ``` Split Measure data file MDATA5 without specifying any output data file names or split criterion. MEASFT attempts to create a two-way split that is as even as possible.
